searchIndex.title: Introduction
searchIndex.description: Pyxle is a Python-first full-stack web framework that brings the Next.js developer experience to the Python ecosystem. You write your server logic in Python, your UI in React, and ship them together i
searchIndex.searchText: Introduction Pyxle is a Pythonfirst fullstack web framework that brings the Next.js developer experience to the Python ecosystem. You write your server logic in Python, your UI in React, and ship them together in a single file — no separate API layer, no second service, no glue code between them. Status: beta () — see the changelog for the current release. Prerequisites: Python 3.10+ (3.12 recommended) and Node.js 20.19+ with npm — Pyxle drives Vite and React under the hood. See Installation. One file, two languages A file colocates a Python loader (and mutations) with the React component that renders its data. The compiler splits them apart at build time, so the Python runs on the server and the JSX runs in the browser — but you author them as one unit, because they describe one thing: a single route. The loader's return value is the component's prop. There's no , no API route to wire up, no type contract to keep in sync — the seam between frontend and backend simply isn't there. Why Pyxle No API plumbing. A loader feeds props straight into your component; an is an endpoint you call with instead of handwriting + a route. Real React. any React library and use it directly — Pyxle renders genuine React 19 with serverside rendering and hydration, not a Python wrapper around a component library. Convention over configuration. Filebased routing, automatic SSR, and zero config for the common cases. Run and you're shipping. Batteries includable. Official plugins for database, auth, and email, plus hooks for middleware, edge caching, and your own integrations. AIfirst DX. Predictable patterns, strong types, and clear errors make Pyxle one of the easiest frameworks for an AI coding agent to ship a whole feature in a single pass. See Pyxle for AI coding agents. Where to go next New here? Start with Installation, then build your first app in Quick Start. Want the mental model? Read Files, Routing, and Data Loading. Coming from another framework? See how Pyxle stacks up in Pyxle vs. other frameworks. Shipping to production? Read the Deployment guide. Curious how it works inside? Take the tour in the Architecture handbook. What's new Pyxle has grown into a depthfirst fullstack framework: serverside caching, SSG & ISR, streaming SSR, realtime WebSockets (with a crossworker Redis broker for multiprocess serving), Pydanticvalidated actions, observability, background tasks, image optimization, and multiworker serving — plus serving any page as clean Markdown with an index, so AI agents read your app as text. See the full Changelog for what shipped in each release. GitHub: github.com/pyxledev/pyxle Install: PyPI: pypi.org/project/pyxleframework

searchIndex.searchText: Installation Prerequisites Pyxle requires: Python 3.10+ (3.12 recommended) Node.js 20.19+ (for Vite 7, React 19, and SSR). Node 18 is endoflife and no longer supported. npm (ships with Node.js) Verify your setup: Install Pyxle Install Pyxle from PyPI: This installs the CLI and the framework runtime. Confirm it works: Installing in a virtual environment (recommended) What gets installed The package includes: Component Purpose CLI Project scaffolding, dev server, build pipeline and decorators for your files Configuration loading and validation Starlette ASGI web server (installed as a dependency) Uvicorn ASGI server runner (installed as a dependency) Language toolkit powering 's JSX validation (installed as a dependency) Node.js dependencies (React 19, Vite 7, and — if you opt in — Tailwind CSS v4) are installed perproject via they are not global. Troubleshooting Two common failures come from an outofdate toolchain. Both are fixed by upgrading — Pyxle needs Python 3.10+ and Node.js 20.19+. says "No matching distribution found for pyxleframework" Your is running on Python older than 3.10 (the stock on macOS is 3.9). Pyxle ships no wheels for endoflife Pythons, so pip reports "none available." Check your version, then install with a supported interpreter: Install a supported Python (via python.org, , or Homebrew's ) and create the virtual environment with that interpreter: / stops with a Node.js version error If is below v20.19, Pyxle stops with a clear message before starting Vite: Vite 7 — which Pyxle uses to build and serve your React code — requires Node.js 20.19 or newer; older 20.x releases crash at startup. Upgrade Node and rerun: Next steps Create your first project: Quick Start

searchIndex.title: Quick Start
searchIndex.description: Create a working Pyxle app in under 5 minutes.
searchIndex.searchText: Quick Start Create a working Pyxle app in under 5 minutes. Before you start: Pyxle needs Python 3.10+ and Node.js 20.19+ (for Vite 7 and React 19). Run and to check. On the wrong version, or fails with a version error — see Installation → Troubleshooting. 1. Scaffold a new project is interactive — it asks a few questions and generates a project tailored to your answers: Each question is an arrowkey selection with the default highlighted — press Enter to accept it. Tailwind CSS — opt in to Tailwind v4, wired straight into Vite (no , no separate watcher). Decline it and you get a clean baseline where plain CSS and CSS Modules work out of the box. shadcn/ui — sets up shadcn/ui (implies Tailwind), so just works. Import alias — the shorthand for absolute imports of your own modules: instead of a brittle path. is the convention (and what shadcn expects); almost everyone keeps it, so the value input only appears if you choose to customize. This creates a directory with a complete starter project. Noninteractive (CI / scripts) When stdin isn't a terminal, never blocks on a prompt — it uses flags and defaults. Drive it explicitly: Scaffold into the current directory Already made the directory (or ran )? Scaffold in place with — the project name is derived from the directory name: 2. Install dependencies This runs both and . You can also run them separately: lists your app's runtime dependencies (Starlette, Uvicorn, HTTPX). Pyxle itself comes from the in step 1. 3. Start the dev server The console prints a short summary — the local URL, the Vite URL, the route count, and how long startup took — then stays quiet unless something needs your attention: Open http://localhost:8000 in your browser. You should see the Pyxle starter page — a centered card showing the framework version, server time, and a link to edit . Each edit you save prints one concise line. Your serverside output also streams to the browser devtools console (prefixed ) so you can follow loaders and actions without switching windows. Need the full picture — raw Vite logs and debug internals? Run . See the CLI reference for details. CSS just works: Vite compiles every stylesheet you import — plain CSS, CSS Modules, and (if you enabled it) Tailwind v4 via the plugin — with hot reload. There's nothing separate to start. See the Styling guide. What just happened? When you ran , the framework: 1. Compiled split the Python server code from the React JSX 2. Started Vite the JavaScript bundler that serves your React components with hot reload 3. Started Starlette the Python ASGI server that handles routing, SSR, and API requests 4. Ran the loader fetched data on the server and passed it as props to React 5. Rendered HTML on the server sent fullyrendered HTML to the browser (SSR) 6. Hydrated on the client React took over the serverrendered HTML for interactivity 4. Make a change Open in your editor. Change the returned by : Save the file. The browser reloads automatically with your updated message. 5. Check your routes This prints the route table derived from your directory: Paths are shown relative to . 6. Validate your project This validates your files — Python syntax, JSX, and semantic issues like undefined names — plus your config file, and reports any problems it finds. Everything Pythonside ships with , and the JSX pass runs on the Node.js install you already have from step 1 — so on a fresh project you should see: Next steps Understand what each file does: Project Structure Learn the file format: Files Add a new page with data loading: Data Loading

searchIndex.title: .pyxl Files
searchIndex.description: A `.pyxl` file is the fundamental building block of a Pyxle application. It combines Python server logic with a React component in a single file -- your data fetching and your UI live together.
searchIndex.searchText: Files A file is the fundamental building block of a Pyxle application. It combines Python server logic with a React component in a single file your data fetching and your UI live together. Anatomy of a file A file has two sections: The compiler finds the boundary automatically: it grows the largest region that parses as valid Python (using Python's own AST), and treats whatever isn't valid Python as JSX. There are no separator comments or directives to write. The Python section The Python section runs entirely on the server. It can: Import modules any Python package available in your environment Define a loader an async function that fetches data for the component Define mutations async functions callable from the client Rules for the Python section One loader per file. The loader receives a Starlette and must return a JSONserializable dict. Multiple functions are allowed. Each becomes a callable endpoint. The function must be . Pyxle enforces this at compile time. Use any Python imports. Standard / statements stay in the Python section because they parse as valid Python. (A JavaScript import such as isn't valid Python, so it is correctly treated as part of the JSX section — the split is by what parses, not by the leading keyword.) The and decorators are available globally you do not need to import them (the compiler injects the import automatically). An optional directive caches the rendered page for seconds (serverside, with incremental regeneration). See Caching. The JSX section The JSX section is a standard React component. It runs on both the server (for SSR) and the client (for hydration and interactivity). Rules for the JSX section Must have a default export. The default export is the page component. Receives as props. The prop contains whatever the loader returned. If there is no loader, is an empty object . Can import from . This gives you , , , , , , , , and . Can import from . Any npm package in your is available. Cannot import Python code. The Python and JSX sections are compiled separately. Must be a pure function of its props. No data fetching, I/O, secrets, Node builtins, side effects, or nondeterministic values (, ) in the render body — load in , mutate in , and put browseronly or effectful code in / event handlers / . See Component purity below. Component purity A component's render is a pure function of its props: given the same , it produces the same HTML, with no side effects. Whatever runs while the page renders on the server must be deterministic. Don't, in the render body: fetch data, query a database, or touch the filesystem / network — that's the loader's job read secrets or import Node builtins (, , , , …) mutate state or cause other side effects use nondeterministic values like , , or — they differ between the server render and the client and cause hydration mismatches Do instead: You want to… Use load data for the page a loader (runs before render; the result arrives as ) change data an , called with or run code after mount, or anything browseronly , an event handler, or This isn't a stylistic preference — purity is what makes a component hydrationsafe (server and client render the same thing), testable (render is a function you can assert on), and fast (no hidden perrender I/O). It also keeps rendering portable to faster, sandboxed SSR backends in the future. Effects and event handlers are exempt — they don't run during the server render. Purity is about the render pass, not the whole component. Controlling the document Use the component from to control what goes in the document : Anything inside is extracted during SSR and inlined into the document . It supports dynamic values via normal JSX interpolation: Head values are automatically sanitised to prevent XSS injection angle brackets inside text are escaped, event handler attributes are stripped, and URLs are neutralised. Note: Pyxle also supports a lowerlevel Python variable for the rare cases where you want fully static head metadata extracted at compile time. For everyday pages, prefer the component. See Head Management for both mechanisms and when to use each. A complete example JSXonly files If a page has no server logic, you can write a JSXonly file: Next steps Learn how files map to URLs: Routing Add data fetching: Data Loading

searchIndex.title: Routing
searchIndex.description: Pyxle uses **file-based routing**. The file structure inside your `pages/` directory determines the URL routes of your application. No router configuration is needed.
searchIndex.searchText: Routing Pyxle uses filebased routing. The file structure inside your directory determines the URL routes of your application. No router configuration is needed. Basic routes Every file in becomes a route: File URL The filename is special it maps to the parent directory's path. Dynamic segments Wrap a filename segment in square brackets to create a dynamic route parameter: File URL Pattern Example The notation above is illustrative. The actual registered path uses Starlette's (and for catchalls) placeholders — this is what prints. Access dynamic parameters in your loader via : Parameter name rules Hyphens in parameter names become underscores: The parameter name is sanitised to be a valid Python identifier Catchall routes Use to match any number of path segments: File URL Pattern Example Matches , The parameter captures the entire remaining path as a string: Optional catchall routes Use (double brackets) to match the parent path and any subpaths: File URL Pattern Example Matches and , , This creates two routes: one for the exact parent path and one for the catchall. Route groups Wrap a directory name in parentheses to exclude it from the URL: Route groups let you organise files by concern without affecting URLs. API routes Python files under become API endpoints: File URL API routes must live under the toplevel directory; use to nest within the API tree. A file placed elsewhere under is ignored — it becomes neither an API route nor a page. API routes are standard Python files (not ). See API Routes for details. Viewing your route table Run to see all registered routes: Special files These filenames have special meaning and do not create their own routes: File Purpose Wraps sibling and descendant pages in a shared layout Like layout but resets state on navigation Error boundary for sibling and descendant pages 404 page for the current directory scope See Layouts and Error Handling. Next steps Load data for your pages: Data Loading Wrap pages in shared layouts: Layouts

searchIndex.title: Data Loading
searchIndex.description: Pyxle uses `@server` decorated functions to load data on the server before rendering a page. The loader runs on every request, fetches whatever the page needs, and passes the result as props to the Re
searchIndex.searchText: Data Loading Pyxle uses decorated functions to load data on the server before rendering a page. The loader runs on every request, fetches whatever the page needs, and passes the result as props to the React component. Basic loader The function: 1. Receives a Starlette [](https://www.starlette.io/requests/) object. Its first parameter must be named exactly (enforced at compile time) 2. Must be (enforced at compile time) and declared at module scope 3. Must return a JSONserializable 4. The return value is available as in the React component 5. Only one function is allowed per file Accessing request data The parameter is a full Starlette . You have access to: Returning status codes Return a tuple of to set the HTTP status: Caching the render For a page whose content is the same for every visitor and changes rarely, return a envelope instead of a plain dict. Pyxle caches the rendered HTML and serves it back without rerunning the loader, refreshing it in the background once it goes stale: is the props your component receives; is the freshness window in seconds. Only do this for pages that render no peruser data — a cached render is shared with every visitor. See Caching for invalidation, incremental regeneration, and the full contract. The envelope is recognized only in its exact twokey shape () and when is itself a dict/mapping. To cache a list, wrap it: — a toplevel list () is not treated as an envelope and is passed through as ordinary props. must be or a nonnegative number of seconds; a bool, a negative number, or a string raises a loader error. Error handling in loaders Raise to trigger the nearest error boundary: When is raised: 1. Pyxle searches up the directory tree for the nearest 2. If found, it renders the error boundary, passing the error context on the prop (not ) 3. If not found, a default error page is shown The boundary therefore destructures , and the context keys are , (camelCase), , and optional (present only when the carried nonempty ): See Error Handling for full details. Using external APIs Loaders can call any Python code databases, APIs, file systems: Calling blocking libraries Loaders are async and run on the server's event loop. Calling a blocking library directly — a sync database driver, , a sync SDK — stalls every other request handled by that worker while the call runs. Wrap blocking calls in so they execute on a worker thread: If the blocking work is a database read, keep one persistent connection per worker thread (for example in a ) instead of connecting per request connection setup usually costs more than the query itself. For API routes there is a second option: a plain is dispatched through Starlette's threadpool automatically. See Sync endpoints and blocking calls. Pages without loaders If a page has no function, is an empty object: How it works 1. A request hits a page route (e.g., ) 2. Pyxle imports the compiled Python module and runs the function 3. The return value is serialised to JSON 4. The React component is rendered on the server with as props 5. The full HTML is sent to the browser 6. React hydrates the page on the client, using the same props embedded in the HTML By default the loader runs on every request. To cache the rendered page and skip the loader on later requests, return a envelope see Caching. Next steps Mutate data with server actions: Server Actions Handle errors gracefully: Error Handling

searchIndex.title: Server Actions
searchIndex.description: Server actions let your React components call Python functions on the server. They are the mutation counterpart to `@server` loaders -- loaders read data, actions write data.
searchIndex.searchText: Server Actions Server actions let your React components call Python functions on the server. They are the mutation counterpart to loaders loaders read data, actions write data. Defining an action Use the decorator on an async function in your file: The function: Must be Receives a Starlette object Must return a JSONserializable Can only be called via request Multiple actions can exist in the same file alongside a loader. Calling actions from React Using the component The simplest way to call an action from a form. collects the inputs, posts them to the action, and exposes / callbacks: props: Prop Type Description Name of the function Override which page the action belongs to (defaults to current page) Called with response data on success Called with the error message and, for a validation failure, the perfield errors () — otherwise Reset form fields after success (default: ) Using the hook For programmatic calls (not form submissions), use the hook: returns an async function with attached state: Property Type Description while the request is in flight Error message on failure, otherwise Perfield validation errors from the last failed submit (a ), or . Cleared when a new request starts Last successful response data The value you is the flat result — on success — so read returned fields directly off it (), not under . A successful result has no . The property in the table above is the hook's convenience copy of that same payload, for reading it outside the call site — don't confuse the two. Options: Option Type Description Override which page the action belongs to Called immediately before the request (for optimistic updates) Error handling in actions Raise to return a structured error response: Since 0.3.0, is autoimported by the compiler for any file that declares at least one . No explicit import is required. A userdefined class (or an existing import) is respected and takes precedence. The client receives: Validating request bodies with Pydantic Reading and pulling fields out by hand means writing the same "is this present, is it the right type, is it in range" checks in every action. Pyxle can do that for you: annotate a parameter with a Pydantic model and the dispatcher parses, coerces, and validates the request body before your action runs. Your function only executes with a valid, fullytyped model. The body parameter: Is the first parameter (after ) annotated with a Pydantic subclass. The name is up to you (, , , …). May be / for an optional body. Is parsed from the JSON request body. submissions (which post form fields) validate the same way. Pydantic is an optional dependency. Install it with the extra: If an action declares a required Pydantic body but Pydantic isn't installed, the request fails with a clear (and the dev server logs why) — actions without a model parameter are unaffected. An optional body ( with a default) silently skips validation when Pydantic is absent: the action runs with the body never validated and the parameter left at its default. What the client receives on a validation failure When the body fails validation, the action is not called. The client gets a response with a toplevel map — field path to a list of messages: Field paths are dotted for nested models () and indexed for list items (). Wholebody errors — a malformed JSON body, or a rootlevel model error — use the key rather than a field name; render it as a formlevel message. Showing field errors in the UI Both and surface the map so you can render messages next to each input. With , read (or the reactive ): With , the field map is the second argument to : Raising validation errors yourself For checks Pydantic can't express — uniqueness, crossfield rules, anything that needs a database — raise . It produces the same shape, so the client handles modellevel and handrolled errors identically: is autoimported alongside in any file that declares an . See the Runtime API reference. Exporting an OpenAPI schema Because the body models are real Pydantic models, Pyxle can generate an OpenAPI 3.1 document describing every action's request body straight from your code: See the CLI reference for options. Invalidating client caches after a mutation Pyxle's client router caches loader payloads (for 2 minutes by default, or the route's configured TTL) so back/forward navigation is instant. A mutation that changes data the user might navigate back to should invalidate the stale route so the next visit refetches: Pyxle attaches an header to the response. and honour the header automatically — neither the calling component nor the page component needs to know about it. For purely clientdriven invalidation (no server roundtrip), use [](../reference/clientapi.md#invalidateurl) from . Running work after the response To do work without making the client wait — send an email, emit a webhook — schedule it on . Pyxle runs it after the response is sent: For a single task you can return the shorthand instead. For fireandforget work that isn't tied to this response (including from loaders), use [](../guides/backgroundtasks.md#fireandforgetworkpyxletasks). See the Background Tasks guide. How actions are routed Each function gets an automatic endpoint: For example, in is available at: You do not need to know these URLs and resolve them automatically. Calling an action directly (scripts and tests) App code never needs the URL, but a script or test can POST to it directly. Send a JSON body; the response is the action's return dict (with ), or when it raises an : CSRF protection is on by default, so a raw like this is rejected with unless you either exempt the action path in [](../reference/configuration.md#csrf) () or send a valid header matching the CSRF cookie ( by default, e.g. — see Security). CSRF protection Actions are protected by CSRF middleware by default. The component and hook handle token management automatically. See Security for details. Route policies on actions Actions run through the same routehook pipeline as pages and API routes, so an auth or ratelimit policy can apply to POSTs. Register action hooks under [](../reference/configuration.md#routemiddleware) — an action hook should reject with a JSON / (not an HTML redirect, which a page hook might use). See the Middleware guide. Next steps Wrap pages in layouts: Layouts Handle errors with boundaries: Error Handling

searchIndex.title: Layouts
searchIndex.description: Layouts wrap pages in shared UI -- navigation bars, sidebars, footers, and other elements that persist across page navigations.
searchIndex.searchText: Layouts Layouts wrap pages in shared UI navigation bars, sidebars, footers, and other elements that persist across page navigations. Creating a layout Add a file to any directory inside . It wraps all pages in that directory and its subdirectories: A layout is a React component that receives : Slots Slots let a page (or a nested layout) inject content into a named placeholder a layout renders. There are two halves: 1. A layout renders a — the placeholder. 2. A page fills it by exporting . The layout renders the placeholder. Import from , give it a , and optionally a : The page fills the slot. Export a object whose values are factory functions that return JSX (): Now the slot in the layout renders the page's instead of the fallback, and reverts to the fallback on pages that don't export it. props: Prop Type Description (required) The slot name to render. Rendered when no filler exists. Defaults to . Forwarded to each filler factory (and to a function ). Rules to know: Each value must be a function returning JSX. A nonfunction value is silently ignored. is optional. A static is enough. Use only when a slot's content depends on the component's props/loader data — it receives the same props as the component () and returns the slots map. Multiple fillers of the same slot name are additive — if a page and a nested layout both fill , all of them render (not lastwins). Advanced: and (both from ) let a layout react to whether a slot is filled — e.g. returns the array of filler factories (or ), so you can hide a toolbar wrapper when nothing fills it. Nesting layouts Layouts nest automatically. If both and exist, a page at is wrapped by both: Inner layouts are rendered inside outer layouts. The root layout is always the outermost wrapper. Templates A file works like a layout but resets component state on every navigation. Use templates when you want a fresh React component tree for each page in the group: Templates are useful for authentication flows, wizards, or any section where you want form state and scroll position to reset when moving between pages. Layout vs template Behaviour Wraps child pages Yes Yes Preserves state on navigation Yes No (remounts) Can nest Yes Yes Typical use Nav bars, sidebars Auth flows, wizards Layout data loaders A layout (or template) can declare its own loader, just like a page. This is the clean way to load data the layout itself needs on every page it wraps a nav bar, a signedin user/session banner, the current theme, the framework version, etc. without repeating it in every page's loader. The layout component receives its loader's result on the prop exactly like a page receives its loader's data. (The same data is also available on , the aliasfree name the page component reads; either prop works inside a layout.) Details: The loader runs once per request, before the page renders, and must be (same rules as a page loader). In a nesting chain, every layout/template loader runs and their results are merged into one dict (on a key conflict, the outermost layout wins). A layout without a loader receives the wrapped page's instead, so existing JSXonly layouts are unchanged. The merged layout data is also exposed to the page component as a prop, if a page ever needs to read what its layouts loaded. How it works When Pyxle compiles your pages, it: 1. Walks up from each page to the root, collecting and files 2. Generates a composed wrapper module that nests them in the correct order 3. At render time, the page component is passed as to the innermost layout Next steps Style your layouts (plain CSS, CSS Modules, or optin Tailwind): Styling Add navigation between pages: Client Components

searchIndex.title: Pyxle vs. other frameworks
searchIndex.description: Pyxle isn't the only way to build a full-stack app in Python, and it isn't the right tool for every job. This page is an honest map of the landscape — what each alternative is genuinely great at, and
searchIndex.searchText: Pyxle vs. other frameworks Pyxle isn't the only way to build a fullstack app in Python, and it isn't the right tool for every job. This page is an honest map of the landscape — what each alternative is genuinely great at, and when you should reach for it instead of Pyxle. The short version: Pyxle's niche is real React UI plus real Python server logic, in one file and one service, with conventions predictable enough that an AI coding agent can ship a whole feature in a single pass. If you never want to write JavaScript, if you're building a data dashboard, or if you need a decademature ecosystem at largeteam scale today, one of the tools below is probably a better fit — and we'll say so plainly. At a glance UI you write Languages Services to run Real React ecosystem Sweet spot Pyxle React / JSX Python + JS one ✅ full React apps, solo & small teams, AIassisted dev Next.js + FastAPI React / JSX Python + TS two ✅ full Large teams, maximum ecosystem & scale Reflex Python Python one ⚠️ wrapped Teams that would rather not touch JS Django Templates (or + React SPA) Python (+ JS) one (or two) ❌ (or twoservice) Content / CRUD apps, mature batteries NiceGUI Python Python one ❌ (Vue under the hood) Internal tools & dashboards, fast Streamlit Python Python one ❌ Data / ML dashboards & demos "Real React ecosystem" means you can any React library and use it directly, rather than through a frameworkspecific wrapper. Pyxle vs. Next.js + FastAPI This is the stack Pyxle most directly replaces: a Next.js (React/TypeScript) frontend talking to a FastAPI (Python) backend over an API. It's the gold standard for a reason — both halves are mature, battletested, and have enormous ecosystems, and the separation scales cleanly to large teams who want a hard frontend/backend boundary. The cost is everything in the seam: two languages, two runtimes, two deploys, a handwritten API contract, CORS, and the type drift between your Pydantic models and your TypeScript types. Pyxle collapses that seam. A loader's return value is your component's props; an is the endpoint, called with instead of . One file, one service, no glue. Use Next.js + FastAPI instead when you have a large team that benefits from a strict frontend/backend split, you need the maturity and scale guarantees of the two mostproven tools in their categories, or you're already invested in that stack. Reach for Pyxle when the twoservice tax is pure overhead for your team size and you'd rather ship one thing. Pyxle vs. Reflex Reflex lets you build the entire app — UI included — in pure Python; it compiles your Python components down to a React/Next.js app under the hood. For a team that never wants to write JavaScript, that's a genuinely compelling promise, and Reflex executes it well. The trade is indirection: you're expressing your UI through Reflex's component abstractions rather than React itself, so reaching for an arbitrary React library or a bit of custom client behavior means going through (or around) that layer. Pyxle makes the opposite bet: you write real React/JSX, so the entire npm React ecosystem is available with no wrapper — but you do write some JavaScript. Use Reflex instead when "zero JavaScript, ever" is a hard requirement for your team. Reach for Pyxle when you want the real React ecosystem and are comfortable writing JSX next to your Python. Pyxle vs. Django Django is the batteriesincluded Python web framework: a superb ORM, the legendary admin, auth, migrations, and a vast, mature ecosystem. For serverrendered, content and CRUDheavy applications it's still one of the best choices in any language. But Django's native UI story is serverrendered templates. The moment you want a rich React frontend, you're back to a twoservice setup (Django REST Framework + a separate React SPA) — the very split Pyxle exists to avoid. Use Django instead when you want a mature ORM and admin out of the box, your UI is well served by serverrendered templates, or you're building something contentheavy where its ecosystem shines. Reach for Pyxle when you specifically want a React UI without bolting on a second service to serve it. Pyxle vs. NiceGUI NiceGUI is a delightful way to build web UIs in pure Python (on top of Vue/Quasar and FastAPI). For internal tools, control panels, and dashboards, it's fast to write and gets you a working interface in minutes. Like Reflex, the UI is defined in Python rather than React, which is exactly what makes it quick for tooling — and exactly why it's a less natural fit for a polished, publicfacing product where you want full control over the React component tree. Use NiceGUI instead for internal tools and dashboards you want to stand up quickly in Python. Reach for Pyxle for publicfacing apps where you want a real, handauthored React frontend. Pyxle vs. Streamlit Streamlit is phenomenal at what it's built for: turning a Python data script into an interactive app. For data exploration, ML demos, and analytics dashboards, nothing gets you there faster. Its execution model — the script reruns top to bottom on every interaction — is the source of that magic, and also the reason it's not aimed at generalpurpose web apps with custom routing, finegrained interactivity, and a bespoke UI. Use Streamlit instead for data and ML dashboards and quick analytical apps. Reach for Pyxle when you're building a generalpurpose web application rather than a data tool. What Pyxle doesn't have yet Pyxle is young and deliberately dependencylight, so some things you may expect from a mature React stack aren't built in yet. Here's an honest list and how to bridge each one today: Authoring your client in TypeScript. Pyxle already gives you endtoend Python types (typed loaders and Pydanticvalidated bodies) and consumes typed npm packages, and / validate your code. What's not here yet is writing the client block itself in TSX with types forwarded across the Python↔React boundary. That's the next milestone — see the road to a fullytyped Pyxle. JavaScript is fully firstclass in the meantime. A firstclass test client. You can unittest loaders and actions and write endtoend tests today (see Testing); an inprocess that drives pages and actions without managing a server is on the roadmap. Automatic image byteoptimization and font optimization. The builtin [](buildoptimization.md) gives you responsive , lazyloading, and priority hints (layout parity with ), but byte optimization (resizing/format conversion) is optin via an image loader/CDN, not automatic. There's no equivalent yet — selfhost fonts with and . Internationalization. There's no builtin i18n routing or message system yet. Use a React i18n library (e.g. ) in your client components, with the locale resolved in a loader. None of these block shipping a real app — they're the honest edges of a fastmoving 0.x, listed so you know exactly where you stand. So which should you choose? Choose Pyxle when you want a real React frontend and a Python backend without running two services, you value predictable conventions (for yourself or your AI agent), and you're happy being an early adopter of a framework that's moving quickly. Choose something else when: You never want to write JavaScript → Reflex or NiceGUI You're building a data or ML dashboard → Streamlit You need a mature, proven ecosystem at largeteam scale today → Next.js + FastAPI or Django Your app is contentheavy CRUD and you want an admin + ORM out of the box → Django A fair caveat: Pyxle is 0.x and evolving — APIs may still shift before 1.0. If you need rocksolid stability for something missioncritical shipping next week, pick a mature option or wait for 1.0. But if you're starting something new and the onefile Python + React workflow sounds like how you want to build, that's exactly who Pyxle is for. Curious about the AIagent angle specifically? See Pyxle for AI coding agents. Ready to try it? The Quick Start takes about five minutes.

searchIndex.title: Migrating from Flask or Django
searchIndex.description: This guide maps the concepts you already know from **Flask** and **Django** to their Pyxle equivalents, so you can port an app a route at a time. It assumes you've read [Core Concepts](../core-concept
searchIndex.searchText: Migrating from Flask or Django This guide maps the concepts you already know from Flask and Django to their Pyxle equivalents, so you can port an app a route at a time. It assumes you've read Core Concepts; for the higherlevel "should I use Pyxle at all" framing, see Pyxle vs. the alternatives. The one mental shift In Flask and Django, a request flows view → template → response: a view function fetches data and hands it to a serverside template language (Jinja or the Django Template Language) that renders HTML. In Pyxle, a route is a single file with two halves: a loader (the view's datafetching job) that returns a plain dict, and a React component (the template's job) that receives that dict as its prop and renders with SSR + hydration. Mutations are a third piece — an function — instead of a second POST view. The loader's return value is the component's prop — there is no separate call. The biggest single rewrite in any migration is turning Jinja/DTL templates into React/JSX. Project layout Flask Django Pyxle , , , , , , , (routes), (JSON), (static), , There is no application object you instantiate ( / ). The tree is the app; the framework assembles the ASGI app (Starlette under the hood) for you. Run to scaffold the layout, then to see the URL map your files produce. See Project structure. Routing Flask / Django Pyxle / → / → catchall converters (catchall), (optional catchall) Blueprints / + folders under ; a folder groups files without adding a URL segment / / no reverserouting helper — use literal ; action URLs are autoresolved Routing is convention, not registration: there is no or route decorator to keep in sync. See Routing. Two honest caveats: No type converters. A param arrives as a string in ; cast it yourself in the loader. There is no equivalent. No . You write literal path strings in / . Form and action endpoints are resolved automatically by / , so you never handwrite those URLs. Views and data loading A Flask/Django view does two jobs — fetch data and render a response. In Pyxle those split: is the Starlette : , , , , , . A loader returns a JSONserializable dict (or a tuple). Blocking calls (a sync DB driver, ) should be wrapped in so they don't stall the event loop. See Data loading. Templates → JSX (the big rewrite) There is no Python template engine in Pyxle. Jinja and the Django Template Language become React/JSX in the same file: Jinja / DTL JSX / a wrapping (and named ) a React component you import import the CSS from JSX (Vite hashes it) or reference at This is real React 19, serverrendered and hydrated — not a stringtemplating layer. Page /meta come from the variable or the component, not a . See .pyxl files, Layouts, and Head management. Forms and mutations Flask / Django Pyxle POST view + + redirect in the same or the hook WTForms / Django + a Pydantic model annotation on the action param CSRF token ( / ) builtin, on by default, handled automatically by / / no flash store — surface success/error as React state Annotating an parameter with a Pydantic model parses, coerces, and validates the body before the action runs, returning a with a map (field → messages) that and surface next to inputs. For checks Pydantic can't express, raise . Actions are POSTonly and autorouted at — you never write that URL. See Server actions. There is no / messages framework: render feedback from 's / or 's / state. JSON APIs A pure JSON endpoint (no UI) is a file under : Flask / Django REST Framework Pyxle / return a Starlette from classbased views / DRF a Starlette subclass (permethod handlers) DRF serializers Pydantic models on actions; emits an OpenAPI 3.1 doc from them There is no DRFstyle router/viewset abstraction — endpoints are plain Starlette handlers. See API routes. The data layer Pyxle core ships no ORM. The data layer is the firstparty plugin (a separate package, declared in ): Django ORM / FlaskSQLAlchemy pyxledb querysets requestscoped with explicit SQL (//, portable placeholders) declarative models optional SQLAlchemy ORM path () / checksumtracked migration files applied at startup (or Alembic for the ORM path) Django admin no equivalent — build CRUD UI yourself Honest gap: there is no Djangostyle declarativemodel + manager layer in core, and no autogenerated admin. If a builtin admin or ORM is a hard requirement, the comparison guide is candid that Django remains the better fit. The closest match is pyxledb's SQLAlchemy ORM path. See the pyxledb plugin docs. Config and environment Flask / Django Pyxle / (debug, host/port, middleware, CORS, CSRF, cache, rateLimit, observability, plugins) (listed in start order) the plugin's block , envspecific config files + envvar overrides; secrets stay in , never in committed config Config is JSON + environment, not a Python config object, and unknown keys are rejected at startup. See Configuration and Environment variables. Middleware, auth, and crosscutting concerns Flask / Django Pyxle /, applevel Starlette middleware in ( strings) perview decorators routelevel hooks under / Django sessions for perrequest data; real sessions via the plugin , guards (/, ), , the client hook CORS / CSRF / ratelimit addons builtin , , , config blocks Middleware is ASGI/Starlette, not WSGI. See Middleware and the pyxleauth plugin docs. Errors and 404s Flask / Django Pyxle / raise (or ); the nearest renders it custom 404 page (directoryscoped, like ) is also a clientside React error boundary, so a render fault after hydration shows the same page rather than a blank screen. See Error handling. Static files and styling is served at the root URL ( → ) — no , no . App CSS/JS is better imported from JSX so Vite bundles and contenthashes it; produces hashed assets and a manifest. Plain CSS and CSS Modules work out of the box; Tailwind v4 is one prompt away at . See Styling. Background work and signals There is no signal/dispatcher bus (, Flask signals). For work after a mutation, schedule it explicitly: , the action shorthand, or for fireandforget. See Background tasks. What has no direct equivalent Be honest with yourself before porting — these have no dropin Pyxle replacement: Django admin — no autogenerated CRUD UI. Reverse URL routing ( / / ) — write literal paths. Flash / messages — surface feedback as React state. A core ORM with declarative models — use the pyxledb plugin (explicit SQL or SQLAlchemy). Serverside HTML templating (Jinja/DTL) — rewrite templates as JSX. Signals / event bus — use explicit background tasks. Typed URL converters () — cast string params yourself. Widgetrendering form classes (WTForms/Django widgets) — author inputs in JSX; validate with Pydantic. A migration checklist 1. a new project alongside your existing app. 2. Move config into + . Host/port/debug/dir settings have overrides; secrets like Django's become plain environment variables read directly (e.g. , used by cookie signing and the plugin), not keys. 3. Port one route at a time: the view's datafetch → a loader; the template → a React component; POST handling → an . 4. Wire the database via the plugin; port models to explicit SQL or the SQLAlchemy ORM path, and migrations to SQL files / Alembic. 5. Add auth via the plugin ( guards, , ). 6. Run and to verify the URL map and catch issues. 7. and for production. Next steps Understand the file format: .pyxl files See the full picture of when to choose Pyxle: Comparison Deploy your migrated app: Deployment

searchIndex.title: Styling
searchIndex.description: Pyxle delegates all CSS to **Vite**. Import a stylesheet from a JSX module and Vite compiles, bundles, hot-reloads (in dev), and content-hashes it (in build) — exactly like it does for JavaScript. You
searchIndex.searchText: Styling Pyxle delegates all CSS to Vite. Import a stylesheet from a JSX module and Vite compiles, bundles, hotreloads (in dev), and contenthashes it (in build) — exactly like it does for JavaScript. You never handbump a query string to bust a cache, and there's no separate CSS build step to run. Three things work out of the box in every project, with or without Tailwind: Plain CSS — CSS Modules — (locally scoped, hashed class names) Any npm CSS — Tailwind CSS is optin — choose it at (or add it later). The default: plain CSS + CSS Modules A project scaffolded without Tailwind ships a plainCSS baseline. The starter imports a global stylesheet and demonstrates a CSS Module: CSS Module class names are deterministic and identical on the server and the client, so the serverrendered HTML and the hydrated React tree always agree — no hydration mismatch. In a production build each stylesheet becomes a contenthashed asset () that the SSR template links automatically. Tailwind CSS (optin, v4, Vitenative) Enable Tailwind when you scaffold: That gives you Tailwind v4, wired directly into Vite via the [](https://tailwindcss.com/docs/installation/usingvite) plugin. There is no , no , and no standalone process — Tailwind runs inside Vite's normal CSS pipeline, so it hotreloads in dev and is hashed in build like any other stylesheet. The entire Tailwind setup is a single CSS entry: imported once from a JSX module (the scaffold imports it from ). Use utility classes anywhere: Tailwind v4 autodetects your content — it scans the files Vite processes, so there's no glob to maintain. Customise your theme with in the same CSS file; see the Tailwind v4 docs. Adding Tailwind to an existing project If you scaffolded without Tailwind and want it later: Pyxle detects in your and adds the plugin to the Vite config it generates. Then replace your CSS entry's contents with (keep any custom rules below it) and restart . shadcn/ui Enable it at scaffold time (, which implies Tailwind). See the Thirdparty packages guide for the full, verified walkthrough. Using another CSS library Any CSS or CSSinJS library that works with React 19 and SSR works with Pyxle. Install it with npm and import it in your JSX section — Vite handles the rest. CSSinJS libraries (styledcomponents, Emotion, etc.) install the same way — and import in the JSX section. Pick libraries with SSR support so server and clientrendered markup match. Legacy Tailwind v3 projects Projects that handwired Tailwind v3 — a at the root, a npm script, and a compiled stylesheet in — keep working: starts the standalone Tailwind watcher when it detects that shape, and runs the declared script. Two things to know: Files under are served live from disk and are never part of the rebuild watch, so the compiled CSS updating in place never triggers reloads — refresh the browser to pick it up. The Tailwind v3 CLI's mode can skip its initial write when its output is piped (nonTTY). If styles look missing under a process manager, run the build script once manually — or better, migrate to the v4 setup above (), which has neither issue. Global stylesheets (configdriven, inlined) For CSS that should be inlined on every page (embedded in the SSR HTML, no separate request), register it in : Paths are relative to the project root; styles are inlined as tags in order, so they apply before JavaScript loads. Use this for tiny critical CSS — for anything substantial prefer the JSXimport path so Vite can hash and cache it. Global scripts Register JavaScript loaded on every page: Next steps Manage document head elements: Head Management Add scripts with loading strategies: Client Components Install packages and set up shadcn/ui: Thirdparty packages
headings: The default: plain CSS + CSS Modules
Adding Tailwind to an existing project

searchIndex.title: Third-party packages
searchIndex.description: A Pyxle project has **two** dependency sets: Python packages (used by your `@server` loaders, `@action` mutations, and `pages/api/*.py` endpoints) and Node packages (used by your React/JSX components)
searchIndex.searchText: Thirdparty packages A Pyxle project has two dependency sets: Python packages (used by your loaders, mutations, and endpoints) and Node packages (used by your React/JSX components). Add to whichever side you need. Python packages (pip) Add the package to and install: Import it in the Python section of a file (above the first JS ) or in a module: Node packages (npm) Install with npm and import it in the JSX section of a file: Vite bundles it for the browser and Pyxle's SSR runtime bundles it for the server, so most packages "just work" on both. Prefer packages with SSR support (they render the same markup on the server and client) — a browseronly package should be used inside a , an event handler, or a boundary. See Client Components. Run both installers at once with . CommonJS packages and SSR The server render bundles your page as an ES module, with React provided by the runtime rather than bundled in. Pyxle resolves dependencies ESMfirst (it prefers a package's /ESM entry over its CommonJS ), so libraries that ship both — including and most of the shadcn/ui ecosystem — render on the server with no extra configuration. A package that ships only CommonJS and calls internally can't be linked into the ESmodule server bundle. If one does, the server render fails with an actionable error that names the package's and your page file, and suggests the fix: use a version that ships an ES module, or render that part of the page clientonly with so it never runs during the server render. The import alias sets up an import alias (default ) in , and Pyxle wires the same alias into the Vite config and the SSR runtime. So resolves to from anywhere, on both server and client: shadcn/ui shadcn/ui is a collection of components you copy into your project (not an installed dependency). Enable it when you scaffold: The scaffold preconfigures everything shadcn needs — , (the alias), , and a Tailwind v4 stylesheet with the shadcn theme tokens — so you don't need to run . Add components directly: This drops into your project (JavaScript, not TypeScript). Import it via the alias and use it in any page: bundles it for production and it renders serverside like any other component. Verified flow. The steps above were verified end to end on Node 22 with : scaffold with → → → import via → + . Because the scaffold ships a ready , running is unnecessary — it would only offer to overwrite the config the scaffold already wrote. Adding shadcn to an existing project If you scaffolded without it, enable Tailwind first (see Styling), then create a at your project root ( can generate it in JavaScript mode — answer no to TypeScript), pointing its at your CSS entry () and its aliases at your import alias. Next steps Styling and Tailwind: Styling Browseronly libraries: Client Components Validate action bodies with Pydantic: Server Actions
headings: Adding shadcn to an existing project

searchIndex.title: Head Management
searchIndex.description: Pyxle offers two ways to control the document `<head>`: the `<Head>` component (**recommended**) and the `HEAD` Python variable. Both merge together with automatic deduplication, and either works — bu
searchIndex.searchText: Head Management Pyxle offers two ways to control the document : the component (recommended) and the Python variable. Both merge together with automatic deduplication, and either works — but we recommend for almost every real page. TL;DR — use the component That's the pattern you'll use for the vast majority of pages. It reads like React, interpolates props naturally, and lives next to the body markup that depends on the same data. Why is the recommended approach It's just JSX. If you know React, you already know how to use it. No new concepts, no callablevsstring rules to memorise. Dynamic content is effortless. You can interpolate , map over arrays, use conditionals, extract to subcomponents — everything JSX lets you do with the body works in the head too. Colocation. Your sits right next to the that uses the same data. Refactoring one updates the other in the same diff. Works in nested components. Any component in your render tree can contribute head elements. A component can set its own ; an wrapper can add . Plays well with layouts. Layouts can set defaults with their own , and pages override them automatically through Pyxle's deduplication rules. Familiar to developers coming from other frameworks. The API is intentionally similar to Next.js's , Remix's export, and React Helmet. The component Import it from and drop it anywhere in your component tree. Its children become elements in the document : The component: Renders nothing in the DOM (it returns ). During SSR, Pyxle extracts its children at compile time and registers them as head elements for the response. Works in any component, including nested ones. A reusable component can inject its own head metadata. Supports head elements: , , , , . Anything outside this allowlist is dropped by the head sanitiser — notably , which is rejected because a stray can rewrite every relative URL on the page (an XSS/redirection vector). Normalises multipart children since 0.3.0. compiles to multiple children — — which React warns about. joins string and number children into a single text node so the warning is silenced and the rendered HTML is unchanged. You don't need template literals or ${name} — My Blog\ workarounds. Multiple blocks in one tree You can use multiple times — it's not a singleton. Elements from all blocks are collected and merged: This lets you put head contributions close to the code that decides them, without having to lift that logic all the way up to the page root. Using in reusable components Head contributions from any rendered component get merged into the final document: This is the idiomatic way to build SEO presets, thirdparty tracking tags, and theme toggles. The variable (lowerlevel alternative) files can also define a variable in the Python section. This was Pyxle's original head mechanism and still works; the parser extracts it at compile time, before React is involved. For head content that depends on loader data, use a callable: The callable receives the loader's return value as its argument and must return a string or list of strings. When to prefer the variable There are a few narrow cases where the variable is a better fit than : 1. Pages with no React component. A pure APIlike page that returns minimal HTML and wants a static without any clientside JavaScript. 2. Absolute hot paths where the few microseconds of skipping React's head capture matter, and the content is fully static. In practice this matters for approximately no one. 3. You deliberately want the head to be decoupled from the render tree — for example, if a page's head is determined by something the component doesn't need to know about. Unless one of these applies, reach for . XSS safety Both children and strings are parsed and rebuilt through a sanitising allowlist before they reach the document: Only , , , , and are permitted in the head — anything else (, , …) is dropped Every attribute value is HTMLescaped, so a quote inside interpolated data cannot break out of its attribute — building // values from loader data (as in the callable above) is safe Angle brackets (, ) inside text are escaped, and any markup injected after a closing is discarded Event handler attributes (, , etc.) are stripped , , and URLs in // attributes are neutralised is rejected One deliberate exception: the text content of inline and elements is treated as trusted author code and preserved verbatim — never interpolate usersupplied data into inline script or style content. Note that and elements are reserialised selfclosing (), so the output markup may differ cosmetically from the input string. This protects against XSS when interpolating userprovided data into head elements. You should still escape user input as a best practice. Layouts and precedence When multiple sources define the same head element, Pyxle deduplicates them. Later sources override earlier ones. Precedence order (lowest to highest) 1. Layout blocks and layout variable 2. Page variable 3. Page blocks Within the same tier, deeper nesting wins (a in a child component overrides a in a parent component). Example: layout defaults + page overrides When the route renders, the layout's and its description are both overridden by the page's values. The favicon survives because the page doesn't define one. Deduplication rules Element Deduplicated by Tag name (only one title allowed) The attribute The attribute Always one charset Only one canonical + combination The attribute Elements with The key value Everything else Not deduplicated (all instances kept) Manual deduplication keys Use to control deduplication for custom elements that don't have a natural identity attribute: If a layout and a page both define an element with the same , the higherpriority source wins. This is useful for tag managers, featureflag bootstrap scripts, or A/B testing snippets. Default title If no element is provided by any source, Pyxle inserts a default: Next steps Add thirdparty scripts: Client Components Build JSON APIs: API Routes
headings: When to prefer the HEAD variable

searchIndex.title: Caching
searchIndex.description: By default a page's `@server` loader runs on **every request** and the page is rendered fresh each time. For pages whose content is the same for everyone and changes rarely — a marketing page, a docs
searchIndex.searchText: Caching By default a page's loader runs on every request and the page is rendered fresh each time. For pages whose content is the same for everyone and changes rarely — a marketing page, a docs article, a blog post — that work is wasted. Pyxle's page cache stores the rendered HTML and serves it back without rerunning the loader or the SSR render, then refreshes it in the background when it goes stale. The one rule: only cache pages that render no peruser data. A cached render is shared byteforbyte with every visitor. If a page embeds a loggedin user's name, a CSRF token, or anything requestspecific, do not cache it — you would serve one user's page to another. Caching is always optin, exactly so this stays your deliberate choice. Making a page cacheable Return a envelope from your loader instead of a plain dict. is the props your component receives (exactly as before); is how many seconds the cached render stays fresh. That's it. The first request renders and stores the page; requests within the next 60 seconds are served from the cache — no loader, no Node render. The envelope is recognised only in its exact twokey shape ( and , nothing else). A normal loader that happens to return keys named or is never mistaken for a cache directive. Pages without a loader A page with no loader can still opt into caching with a modulelevel directive in its Python section: is the freshness window in seconds, exactly as in the loader envelope ( means "serve cached but rerender every request"). It is validated at compile time: an invalid directive (nonnumeric or negative value, or a malformed shape) is reported as a compile diagnostic and the page is treated as uncached — whether that diagnostic blocks the build depends on your strict/nonstrict diagnostic mode. If a page declares both a loader envelope and a directive, the loader's wins. Incremental regeneration (stalewhilerevalidate) When a cached page passes its window, the next request still gets the cached (stale) bytes immediately — no one waits for a rerender — and a single background rerender refreshes the cache for everyone after it. This is incremental static regeneration (ISR): fast responses, freshenough content, and never a thundering herd (only one refresh runs per page at a time, even under load). is valid and means "serve the cached copy but rerender on every request" — useful when you want to absorb bursts without ever serving content older than one render. Static prerendering () Pages with no loader and no dynamic route parameters render the same HTML for everyone, every time. renders them once at build time and stores the result, so the first request after a deploy is already a cache hit — no cold SSR render, even for the very first visitor. This prerenders every loaderless, nondynamic page into the build output's subdirectory ( by default; here means the configured build/output directory, i.e. ); on startup warms its page cache from that directory. Pages with a loader (or a route) are skipped — they still render live and cache at runtime as before. A static page's layout loader still runs at build time — its result is baked into the prerendered HTML, so a layout loader used by static pages should return the same data for everyone (global status, navigation), not peruser state. Build time loaders run with the same plugin context a request has, so a layout (or page) loader that reads from a plugin like prerenders correctly — the static builder opens your plugins' connections at build time, just as a server would. Prerendered entries have no expiry until you the route. With the default inmemory backend they are rewarmed from the new on every restart; with a shared file/redis backend, warmed copies persist in that store, so if a later deploy stops passing (or adds a loader to a previouslystatic route), flush the cache or invalidate those routes — replacing does not clear them. Invalidating the cache When the underlying data changes — you publish a post, edit a page — purge the cached render so the next request rerenders immediately instead of waiting out the window: purges one route's cached render. Returns if something was cached, otherwise — safe to call either way. purges every cached render. How it interacts with the edge cache A route listed in your [](../reference/configuration.md) block (the edge cache, which sets for a CDN) is also served from the serverside page cache automatically, using that same TTL — you don't need to repeat yourself. When both apply, a loader's wins over the edge . Cached page responses carry a strong , so a conditional request () gets a , and an response header reports , , or for debugging. Client navigation cache Separately from the serverside page cache, the browser keeps a perURL navigation cache of loader payloads so back/forward navigation is instant. Its lifetime mirrors a page's real cacheability: A page with a entry (or directive) reuses that TTL. A dynamic page — a loader with no declared cache lifetime — is not navigationcached by default (TTL ), so a fresh navigation always refetches and a justmade mutation is visible immediately rather than hidden behind a stale window. Opt in by giving the route a entry. A static, loaderless page uses the client default (2 minutes). See Navigation cache TTL for the full clientside details. Where the cache lives The cache is enabled automatically for production serves () and disabled in so a cached render never hides an edit while you work. Choose where rendered HTML is stored with the environment variable: Stores in Use when (default) bounded inprocess memory (LRU) single process, or any deploy where perworker caches are fine local disk () one host, multiple workers sharing a cache that survives restarts shared Redis () multiple hosts, or crossworker invalidation nothing — caching disabled you want it off in production ( and are accepted aliases for ) The inmemory backend is bounded by entry count (, default 512) and total body bytes (, default 64 MiB) with LRU eviction, so it never grows without limit. The Redis backend needs the optional extra: . Invalidation and workers. With the inmemory or file backend under , each worker keeps its own store, so reaches the worker that handled the action; entries still expire everywhere on their own via . The Redis backend is shared across every worker and host, so an invalidation fans out to all of them — choose it when you need crossworker purges. When not to cache Pages that show the signedin user's data, a peruser CSRF token, or anything that varies by request. Leave these as plain loaders (), and they are never cached. Pages that must always reflect the absolute latest data with zero staleness — use a plain loader, or plus on every write. Query strings: the cache keys on the route path only, so a request that carries a query string (, ) is always served live — never cached and never served a cached entry. A page whose content varies by query is therefore never collapsed onto one shared render. Next steps Load data: Data Loading Mutate data: Server Actions Configure the edge cache: Configuration
headings: How it interacts with the edge cache

searchIndex.title: Build Optimization
searchIndex.description: `pyxle build` produces a hashed, minified, code-split client bundle and a matching SSR shell. Most of the optimization is automatic; this guide covers what you get for free, how to inspect it, and how
searchIndex.searchText: Build Optimization produces a hashed, minified, codesplit client bundle and a matching SSR shell. Most of the optimization is automatic; this guide covers what you get for free, how to inspect it, and how to push image and load performance further. What you get automatically Pyxle builds the client with Vite in production mode, so out of the box: Minification — JavaScript and CSS are minified with esbuild. Treeshaking — unused exports (including unused helpers) are dropped by Rollup. Code splitting — shared code lands in its own chunks, so two pages that import the same component download it once and cache it across navigations. Content hashing — every asset is fingerprinted () for immutable, longlived caching. Perpage CSS — only the CSS a page actually uses is linked from its shell. Preload hints Pyxle's SSR shell injects a for the page's entry module and every chunk it statically imports, derived from the build manifest's import graph. The browser then fetches those chunks in parallel with HTML parsing instead of discovering them only after parsing the entry — a meaningful firstload win on multichunk pages. It's automatic; there's nothing to configure. (Browsers that don't support simply ignore the hints — the page still loads.) Inspecting the bundle — To see what ships, add : It prints every JS/CSS asset with its raw and gzipped size, largest first, plus a total: Use it to catch a dependency that ballooned a chunk, or to confirm a refactor shrank the bundle. (It's dependencyfree — no extra tooling to install.) Image optimization (from ) is an optimized on par with Next.js's component for everything that doesn't require a serverside image optimizer: Out of the box it: Prevents layout shift — / reserve the right space before the image loads (no CLS). Lazyloads belowthefold images (), and prioritizes the LCP image when you pass ( + eager + sync decode). Supports a blurup placeholder ( + ), an automatic , and mode (cover a positioned parent). Responsive images with a loader Actual resizing and format conversion (WebP/AVIF) need a backend — a CDN or a build plugin. Provide a and emits a responsive across a devicesize ladder; the browser downloads the size it needs: Most CDN loaders also do format negotiation (serving AVIF/WebP based on the header) and compression, so a single gives you resizing and modern formats. imgix, Cloudflare Images, Vercel, and ImageKit all fit the same shape. Why no default resizing backend? Without one, a would just point at the original image at every width — the browser would download the full image regardless, which is slower, not faster. So Pyxle stays honest: no loader → a clean, CLSsafe ; a loader → real responsive images. Buildtime optimization with a Vite plugin If you'd rather optimize images at build time than at the edge, add a Vite image plugin to your project and reference the optimized output. For example, [](https://github.com/FatehAK/vitepluginimageoptimizer) (compression) or [](https://github.com/JonasKruckenberg/imagetools) (onthefly resizing + generation via import queries). These are optin: install the npm package and add the plugin to your project's Vite config — Pyxle doesn't bundle an imageprocessing dependency. See also reference Deployment → CDN and edge caching Caching
headings: Build-time optimization with a Vite plugin

searchIndex.title: Streaming SSR
searchIndex.description: By default Pyxle renders a page to its complete HTML string and then sends it. For a page with a slow part — a section that waits on a third-party API, a heavy below-the-fold widget — the visitor star
searchIndex.searchText: Streaming SSR By default Pyxle renders a page to its complete HTML string and then sends it. For a page with a slow part — a section that waits on a thirdparty API, a heavy belowthefold widget — the visitor stares at a blank screen until the whole page is ready. Streaming SSR sends the page in pieces. Pyxle flushes the fast part (the "shell") immediately, so the browser can paint it, and streams the slow parts in as they become ready. Timetofirstbyte drops to the time it takes to render the shell instead of the whole page. Streaming is built on React's and is optin: a page streams only when it uses a boundary. Every other page keeps the buffered render, unchanged. Opting in with Wrap the slow part of your page in a boundary and give it a . The fallback renders into the shell and streams immediately; the boundary's real content streams in when it resolves. The compiler detects the at build time and marks the page as streamable — there's no configuration and no flag to set. A page with no is never affected. Import under that exact name. The compiler keys streaming off the literal element name (or ). A page that aliases the import — and writes — compiles fine but is not marked streamable and renders buffered, with no warning. works the way it does in any React app: a child suspends by throwing a promise (via or your own promisethrowing data source), and React shows the until it resolves. Pyxle's loader still runs first and passes its result as props, exactly as for a buffered page — streaming governs how the rendered page is delivered, not how the loader runs. Pyxle ships React 19, so the hook is available if you prefer it. remains the zeroeffort way to make a subtree suspend and stream. Routelevel loading states with A file declares a routelevel loading state — the React component it exports becomes the fallback for the whole route, the way Next.js's does: Pyxle wraps the page in — on the server (so the loading state streams as the shell) and on the client (so hydration matches). The nearest wins, walking up the directory tree exactly like [](errorhandling.md). It composes with a page's own inner : an inner boundary handles its own subtree, and anything it doesn't catch bubbles up to the shell. A is compiled but never routable on its own. A only shows if the render actually suspends. Because the loader runs before the component renders, a page whose data comes entirely from the loader has its props ready by render time and never suspends — so the fallback never appears, and the full page streams at once. The loading state shows only when the render itself suspends: a child using or a thrown promise. This is the honest consequence of Pyxle's loaderfirst model; if you want a loading state for slow loader data, that data isn't what defers. A should not declare a — like any streamed fallback it renders before the head is finalized, and on a clientside navigation the head is updated before the fallback renders, so a fallback's is ignored. Hydration Nothing changes about hydration. The browser hydrates the same component it always did; React reconciles the streamed markup (including the parts that arrived after the shell) natively. A streaming page is hydrated exactly like a buffered one. Hydration is selective: React hydrates the shell (and makes its interactive parts live) without waiting for a boundary to finish — an interactive control above the boundary responds to input while the boundary is still resolving, and each boundary hydrates as its content arrives. Faster timetofirstbyte The point of streaming is that the browser receives — and can paint — the shell before the slow boundary is ready. For a page whose boundary takes, say, 600 ms to resolve, the shell's first byte arrives in tens of milliseconds rather than after the full 600 ms a buffered render would wait. The slower the boundary, the bigger the win; a page with no slow boundary gains nothing, which is exactly why streaming is optin. Concurrent streams don't queue Overlapping requests to a streaming page render concurrently, even against a single SSR worker. A streaming render spends almost all of its wallclock time idle — awaiting your loader and each boundary — and the worker interleaves those idle windows across requests. Four visitors hitting the same slow streaming page all get their shell's first byte in tens of milliseconds; none waits for the others' boundaries to resolve first. Each render is fully isolated: its pathname, CSRF token, tags, and styles are scoped to that request, so interleaving never bleeds one visitor's page into another's. Adding SSR processes (, autosized by default under ) adds CPUparallel throughput on top; the inworker concurrency handles the idlewait overlap. See Deployment → SSR workers. When a page does not stream Streaming is deliberately narrow. A page falls back to the buffered render when: It has no boundary. There's nothing to defer, so there's no shell to flush early. It is publicly cacheable — a loader envelope, a directive, or an edge config entry (see Caching). A cacheable render has to be materialised in full so it can be stored and given an ; streaming it would buy nothing, so cacheable routes always render buffered. Streaming helps the dynamic, perrequest pages that can't be cached. The server is running without an SSR worker pool. Streaming needs the pool's multiframe transport. autosizes the pool by default and runs a single worker, so streaming works out of the box in both. The only way to lose it is , which selects the perrequest subprocess renderer (no pool, no streaming). Under , means autosize (one worker per CPU, capped at 4), not subprocess mode — so it keeps streaming. A streamed response is always — it is a perrequest render and is never shared between visitors. Streaming survives gzip in production Production builds enable gzip compression. Pyxle uses a streamingaware gzip middleware that flushes the compressor after every chunk, so the shell's compressed bytes reach the browser immediately instead of being held back until the whole response finishes. Streaming SSR therefore works shellfirst behind gzip under — no configuration needed, and no need to disable compression to keep the streaming benefit. The head is static while streaming Because the document is flushed before the component renders (that's what makes the shell fast), only the static head is available to a streamed page: the variable (including a callable evaluated from loader data) and blocks declared in your JSX or layout. A element registered during render — i.e. returned from a component as it renders — arrives too late to reach the alreadyflushed head and is omitted from a streamed page. Put meta tags a streamed page needs in the variable, not in a runtimerendered . This only affects pages that opt into streaming; buffered pages merge runtime exactly as before. Custom middleware and streaming A custom middleware that subclasses Starlette's buffers the whole response before passing it on, which is fundamentally incompatible with a streamed render. When a boundary genuinely defers, the page becomes a chunked , and a in the stack raises (This can be intermittent in dev — a warm chunk cache resolves the boundary in a single flush — but it fails every time a boundary defers on real async data, e.g. in production.) If you run streaming routes, write custom middleware as pure ASGI instead of subclassing . A pureASGI middleware passes streamed and buffered responses through identically. See Middleware → Streamingsafe middleware for the pattern. Errors If the render fails before the first byte is sent — an error while producing the shell — Pyxle does not emit a halfwritten document. It falls back to the nearest [](errorhandling.md) boundary (or the sanitized error page), exactly like a buffered render. Once the shell has flushed, an error inside a boundary is handled by React: it streams the boundary's fallback and recovers on the client.
headings: The head is static while streaming

searchIndex.title: WebSockets
searchIndex.description: A Pyxle page can serve a **WebSocket** alongside its HTML. Add a module-level `async def websocket(ws)` to a page's Python section and Pyxle registers a WebSocket route at the page's path — the same f
searchIndex.searchText: WebSockets A Pyxle page can serve a WebSocket alongside its HTML. Add a modulelevel to a page's Python section and Pyxle registers a WebSocket route at the page's path — the same file serves both the rendered page over HTTP and a live connection over WS. Open and you get the page; the client hook upgrades the same path to a WebSocket. Two browsers in exchange messages in real time; is a separate room. Derive the path from props, not . The component renders on the server first, and the hook's arguments are evaluated during that render — only the connection waits for the browser. Reaching for a browser global at render scope () therefore crashes SSR with . Build the path from route data instead, as above: the loader returns the dynamic segment and the component templates it into the same path the page was served from. The handler Detection is by convention, not a decorator: a modulescope coroutine named is the handler. (This matches how modules already expose a callable, and keeps free of new symbols.) It must be at module scope — a or a is a compile error (almost always a mistyped handler). Its first argument is the Starlette [](https://www.starlette.io/websockets/) — name it whatever you like (, , …); it is not a . Dynamic segments resolve into (e.g. for ), exactly like a page loader.
