You call yourself, then read with / and write with / . The handler owns the full connection lifecycle. A page WebSocket handler runs without middleware — no auth population, no CSRF, no route hooks. Starlette runs HTTP middleware only for scope. See Authentication below. Channels (pub/sub) provides a small broadcast layer so you don't handroll connection bookkeeping. subscribes the connection to a named channel for the life of the block (unsubscribing on disconnect), and gives you a handle to to everyone in it: A published message reaches every subscriber, including the sender — a chat UI that doesn't want to echo the sender's own messages filters them clientside. are delivered as binary frames, as text, anything else as JSON. accepts a keywordonly to use an explicit broker for that call — — useful in tests or against a nondefault backend without touching . The broker lives on (one per process). Reach it inside a handler with (the intended accessor) rather than digging into . The default also offers (send to every connection across all channels) and for introspection — extras beyond the core protocol. For a custom backend, implement the protocol ( / / ) and set your own: Multiworker caveat. The default lives in one process. Under , each worker has its own broker — a client on worker 1 never receives a message published on worker 2 (Pyxle logs a warning at startup when this combination is detected). For crossworker realtime, use a shared backend (Redis pub/sub) or stickysession routing at the load balancer. The default is correct for and singleworker . Why you may not see this locally. Workers share one listening socket and the OS loadbalances new connections across them via . Under light, burstybyhand load — opening a couple of browser tabs — the kernel keeps handing consecutive connections to the same "hot" worker, so two tabs almost always land together and you'll see them share state (e.g. a presence count of 2). The split across brokers only shows up reliably under concurrent load. So "it works on my machine with " is not evidence that crossworker realtime is safe — assume separate workers can't see each other and plan a shared broker accordingly. Crossworker realtime with Redis For multiworker (or multimachine) realtime, switch the broker to Redis — no code change, just configuration: Every worker now relays / traffic through Redis pub/sub: a message published on any worker reaches every subscriber on every worker (and every machine pointed at the same Redis), delivered to each connection exactly once. Your handler code is identical — and work the same; only the broker behind changes. The connection is pinged at startup, so a wrong URL fails loudly rather than silently dropping messages, and the listener reconnects automatically if Redis blips. Env var Default Purpose (inprocess) or Redis connection URL namespace for the Redis channels Presence counts are still perprocess unless you share that state too. The broker makes message delivery span workers, but a "users online" counter you keep in a modulelevel dict is perprocess — derive presence from a shared store (e.g. a Redis counter) if you need an exact crossworker count. The Redis broker is the default inprocess broker's dropin replacement (s the namespace and filters locally); for very high channel counts, a perchannel subscription variant is a future optimisation. Authentication in a WebSocket handler Because the auth middleware never runs for a WebSocket upgrade, is not available. Resolve the session yourself with , which reads the session cookie off the handshake and returns the user (or ): It returns (doing zero work) when [](../plugins/pyxleauth.md) isn't installed or no session cookie is present. Gating specific actions on a public socket. A socket can stay open to everyone (readonly) while restricting writes to authenticated users. Call when a privileged frame arrives, and take the identity from the session — never from the client payload, which can be forged: Origin checking. CSRF doesn't apply to a WebSocket upgrade, so a crosssite origin check is the equivalent guard against a hostile page opening a socket with the victim's cookie. Enforce it when the socket carries privileged state: The client hook connects from the browser with autoreconnect, JSON parsing, and connection state. It never connects during SSR — though its arguments are still evaluated there, so derive from props or loader data, never from — and reconnects with exponential backoff (capped, with jitter). Returns Type Description Connection state Send a string asis, or JSONencode anything else; if not open The most recent received message (JSONparsed when possible) The last error message Options: , , (default ), (default ). A relative is resolved against the current origin with the matching scheme ( on ); an absolute / URL passes through. Behind a reverse proxy If you serve Pyxle behind nginx (or any proxy), the proxy must be told to pass the WebSocket upgrade through — otherwise the handshake is proxied as an ordinary request, the page still loads, and every WebSocket silently fails to connect. See Deployment → Reverse proxy setup for the / lines. (Caddy upgrades WebSockets automatically.) See also API routes — modules can export a callable too (and an for HTTP on the same path). pyxleauth — the session the WS auth helper reads. Deployment — multicore serving and the broker caveat.

searchIndex.title: API Routes
searchIndex.description: Files under `pages/api/` are API endpoints. They are plain Python files (not `.pyxl`) that handle HTTP requests and return JSON or other responses.
searchIndex.searchText: API Routes Files under are API endpoints. They are plain Python files (not ) that handle HTTP requests and return JSON or other responses. Basic API route Create . An API module exports an callable that receives the Starlette and returns a response: handles every HTTP method bound to the route. This responds to : HTTP methods receives every method bound to the route. Branch on to handle more than one: For multimethod endpoints with automatic handling, use an class (below) — Starlette dispatches each request to the matching //… method and rejects the rest. Using HTTPEndpoint classes For more structure, use Starlette's : Sync endpoints and blocking calls can also be a plain synchronous function. Pyxle dispatches sync endpoints through Starlette's threadpool, so a blocking body — a database driver, a sync SDK — occupies a worker thread instead of freezing the event loop: The same applies to sync //… methods on classes — Starlette threadpools those natively. Inside an endpoint, never call blocking libraries directly — that stalls every request on the worker's event loop. Either make the endpoint sync (above) or wrap the call: For submillisecond calls the syncendpoint form is usually faster — one threadpool hop per request instead of a hop per wrapped call. Note: route hooks (and the default API policies) wrap function endpoints. classes are dispatched natively by Starlette and bypass route hooks — the same rationale as WebSocket routes. WebSocket endpoints Since 0.3.0, an API module can export to register a WebSocket handler at the same path. The file can export both (HTTP) and — they bind to the same URL and Pyxle dispatches based on the protocol of the incoming request. Client side: You can also export a Starlette subclass for multimethod dispatch: Notes: WebSocket handlers run outside the HTTP routehooks pipeline — hooks wrap requesttoresponse callables and the WS lifecycle doesn't match that shape. Authenticate, ratelimit, and log inside the handler body. CSRF doesn't apply to WebSocket upgrades. Enforce your own origin / session checks in before . Dynamic API routes Use the same bracket syntax as page routes: Reading request bodies Error responses Return appropriate HTTP status codes: API routes vs server actions Feature API routes Server actions File location Inside files HTTP methods Any (GET, POST, PUT, etc.) POST only Response format Any Starlette Response JSON dict Called from Anywhere (curl, fetch, etc.) or CSRF protection On for POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE¹ Enabled by default Use case Public APIs, webhooks, integrations Form submissions, mutations ¹ CSRF runs appwide, so a statechanging API request (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) must carry the doublesubmit token by default — same as any other route. A public webhook or thirdparty integration that can't send the token must list its path prefix in [](security.md). Safe methods (GET/HEAD/OPTIONS) are never checked. Next steps Add middleware to your routes: Middleware Protect routes with CSRF: Security

searchIndex.title: Background Tasks
searchIndex.description: Some work shouldn't make the user wait: sending a welcome email, emitting a webhook, warming a cache. Pyxle gives you two ways to run it off the request's critical path — pick by *when* the work shoul
searchIndex.searchText: Background Tasks Some work shouldn't make the user wait: sending a welcome email, emitting a webhook, warming a cache. Pyxle gives you two ways to run it off the request's critical path — pick by when the work should run. You want… Use Runs Work tied to a response (after this replies) After the response is sent Fireandforget work from anywhere (loader, action, API) On a background worker pool Both run inprocess on the app's event loop. For work that must survive a restart or run exactly once across machines, reach for a real job queue — see below. Postresponse work in an Inside an , is a Starlette [](https://www.starlette.io/background/). Add tasks to it and Pyxle runs them after the response is sent, so the client isn't kept waiting: accepts a coroutine function or a plain function (run in a threadpool). The task runs after the response — a failure in it can't change what the client already received, so log inside the task if you need to know it ran. exists only inside an . A loader or a plain API route never gets it, so raises there — use (below) from a loader or API route instead. The shorthand For a single task, you can return it from the action instead of touching : The value is ; Pyxle strips the key from the response body and schedules to run after the response. The shorthand passes positional args only — there's no way to express keyword args in the flat list. For keyword args or more than one task, use . Fireandforget work — When the work isn't tied to a particular response — a loader that wants to refresh something, an API route emitting an event — enqueue it on the app's inprocess task queue: schedules on a small pool of background workers (coroutine functions are awaited; plain callables run in a thread, so a blocking SDK call won't stall the loop). It returns immediately. A task that raises is logged — it never takes a worker down. The queue is bounded; under sustained overload raises rather than growing without limit. The queue starts and stops with the app, so works inside any request. Called outside a running app it raises . Multiworker caveat. The inprocess queue lives in one process, so under each worker has its own queue, and queued work is lost if that worker restarts. It's the same tradeoff as the inprocess WebSocket broker. For durability or exactlyonce semantics across workers, use a real job queue. Production job queues Pyxle's inprocess tasks are deliberately small — perfect for "send an email after signup," not for a videotranscoding pipeline. When you need retries, scheduling, durability, or workers on separate machines, hand off to a dedicated queue. They slot in cleanly because an is just async Python: Celery — the most common choice. Define a , then call from an action. Run as a separate process. ARQ — asyncnative, Redisbacked. from an action; ARQ's worker runs it. A natural fit since Pyxle handlers are already async. Dramatiq — then . In every case the pattern is the same: the action enqueues a job and returns; the external worker does the heavy lifting. Pyxle doesn't wrap these — use their own client directly so you keep their full feature set. See also Server Actions Runtime API →

searchIndex.title: Middleware
searchIndex.description: Pyxle supports two levels of middleware: **application-level middleware** that wraps every request, and **route-level hooks** that run only for specific route types.
searchIndex.searchText: Middleware Pyxle supports two levels of middleware: applicationlevel middleware that wraps every request, and routelevel hooks that run only for specific route types. Applicationlevel middleware Add Starlettecompatible middleware classes to your config: Each entry is a string in format. The resolved attribute can be any of: a class (a standard Starlette middleware or subclass); an alreadyconstructed Starlette instance; a tuple — the only way to pass constructor kwargs to a configloaded middleware (the class must subclass ); a zeroargument factory callable returning any of the above. Writing a middleware is convenient but buffers the response, so it is incompatible with streaming SSR. If any route streams, use the pureASGI form below. Streamingsafe (pureASGI) middleware A pureASGI middleware passes streamed and buffered responses through identically — it never buffers, so it works with a streaming render. Set headers at by wrapping : This is the streamingsafe equivalent of the example above. Register it the same way (). Middleware is applied in the order listed in config: the first entry is the outermost wrapper among your custom middleware. The whole custom group runs inside the framework's CORS, CSRF, and staticserving layers — see Middleware execution order below. Streaming SSR caveat. A middleware subclassing buffers the whole response, which breaks streaming SSR: when a boundary defers, the page becomes a chunked and raises If you run streaming routes, write custom middleware as pure ASGI instead (see Streamingsafe (pureASGI) middleware below). Routelevel hooks Route hooks run before and after specific route handlers. Configure them per route type: The three lists wrap page handlers, API route functions, and endpoints respectively. They run through the same hook pipeline, so an POST is subject to its own policy chain instead of bypassing it. Actions vs pages — return the right thing. An action endpoint replies with JSON, so an action hook that rejects a request should return a JSON / (not an HTML redirect a page hook might use). That's why actions have a separate list rather than inheriting the page's hooks — protect a page and its actions, but with responses each side can parse. Note: Route hooks wrap function endpoints ( in ), page handlers, and endpoints. classes are dispatched natively by Starlette and bypass route hooks — as do WebSocket handlers — so auth or ratelimiting for classbased endpoints must live in the endpoint methods themselves or in applicationlevel middleware. Writing a route hook (function style) The function receives three arguments: 1. a with metadata about the matched route 2. the Starlette 3. an async callable that invokes the next handler Writing a route hook (class style) RouteContext The object provides metadata about the matched route: Property Type Description Route type URL path pattern File path relative to project root Absolute file path on disk Python import key Hash of the compiled route module — changes when the source changes, stable across reloads Whether the page has a loader Rendered markup registered by the page/layout (SSR only) HTTP methods the route handles is a frozen dataclass — fields are readonly. A shorthand returns a JSONfriendly view (keys camelCased, paths as POSIX strings) that Pyxle attaches to for downstream middleware. Builtin hooks Pyxle applies two default hooks: adds route info to returns 405 for disallowed API methods Rate limiting Pyxle ships a dependencyfree tokenbucket rate limiter, . Enable it from — no code required: Each client gets a bucket holding up to tokens that refills at tokens per second. A request spends one token; when the bucket is empty the request is rejected with and a header. This permits a short burst up to the capacity while bounding the sustained rate — friendlier than a hard fixed window, and it never blocks the event loop. The 429 response carries the JSON body with and an integer (seconds) header, so a client can detect and parse throttling. Client key — the connection's remote IP, or the first hop when is set. Only trust the header behind a proxy you control; otherwise a client can spoof it to dodge the limit. Exempt paths — skips the limiter on segment boundaries (same matching as ). List health checks and metrics scrapes here so monitors aren't throttled. Placement — the limiter sits just inside request observability, so a throttled request is still assigned a correlation id and counted in metrics, but is rejected before CSRF, static serving, or your handler do any work. Multiworker caveat. The bucket store is inmemory and perprocess. Under each worker enforces the limit independently, so the effective global cap is . When you need one shared limit across workers or hosts, ratelimit at your reverse proxy / load balancer instead. See the full schema in the configuration reference. You can also apply it yourself in a custom ASGI stack: Middleware execution order From outermost to innermost: 1. Request observability (correlation id + timing, on by default) 2. Rate limiter (if ) 3. GZip compression + security headers (production only) 4. CORS middleware (if configured) 5. CSRF middleware (if enabled) 6. Static file serving 7. Custom middleware (from config) 8. Plugincontributed middleware 9. Vite proxy (dev mode) 10. Route hooks (from config) — skipped for classes and WebSocket handlers, which Starlette dispatches natively 11. Page/API handler Custom middleware runs inside CORS, CSRF, and static serving — those wrap it. Two consequences: a custom middleware cannot see or modify a CORS or CSRF rejection response (the request was already rejected further out), and it never observes staticasset requests (static serving responds and returns before reaching it). The first middleware in your list is still the outermost among your custom middleware, but the whole custom group sits inside the framework's security and static layers. Next steps Configure environment variables: Environment Variables Add CORS and CSRF: Security

searchIndex.title: Plugins
searchIndex.description: Pyxle plugins are reusable pieces of functionality — database layers, auth systems, storage adapters — that a host app composes via `pyxle.config.json` rather than hand-wiring in its entry point. The
searchIndex.searchText: Plugins Pyxle plugins are reusable pieces of functionality — database layers, auth systems, storage adapters — that a host app composes via rather than handwiring in its entry point. The design is deliberately Djangoinspired: declare what you want, and the framework handles discovery, startup order, and lifecycle. Status (Phase A — stable since 0.5.0). This guide covers the Phase A plugin surface: lifecycle hooks, named services, and middleware contribution. Page contribution (plugins shipping their own pages that get merged into the host's route tree — the "pyxleauth ships a default page" use case) is a Phase B followup, still in RFC. Everything documented here is stable; the additions in Phase B will be strictly additive. TL;DR Declare plugins in : Pyxle imports each plugin, calls its hook inside the ASGI lifespan, and gives your app three ways to reach the services plugins register. Pick whichever reads best — they all resolve to the same object. All three coexist. The pluginprovided helpers (option 1) are preferred for app code because they typeannotate the return value; option 2 is there for adhoc access; option 3 is the fundamental mechanism. Config schema Each entry in is either a string or an object: Field Required Default Description ✅ — Userfacing name. Used for servicenamespace identification and error messages. — Python module where the plugin object lives. — Attribute on to fetch. A subclass is instantiated; an instance is used asis. — Perapp config dict forwarded to the plugin instance via . The string form is sugar for . Both sugar and object form work sidebyside. Authoring a plugin Minimum viable plugin A plugin is a regular Python package that exports a subclass (or instance) named : Ship a typed import helper Every plugin should ship a oneliner helper so consumers can import instead of reaching into the registry: Consumer code then writes — typed, short, idiomatic. See the and helpers for realworld examples. Handling plugin settings Settings arrive as a dict from the host app's . The framework doesn't validate the shape — your plugin should. A simple pattern: The "unknown keys" check is important — a typo like would otherwise silently fall through to the default and leave the user wondering why their setting isn't taking effect. Fail loud at startup, not at runtime. Declaring a dependency on another plugin Plugins declare dependencies in two places: 1. In — so pip installs the dep alongside your plugin. 2. In at servicelookup time — plus a clear error if the required service isn't present (which means the host app forgot to list the dependency in , or listed them in the wrong order). Phase B will introduce declarative dependencies so the framework can toposort plugins for you. For now, order is manual. Environment variables Plugins that read environment variables should document the contract clearly in their README. There's no frameworklevel envvar declaration yet — Phase B adds for style validation. Testing a plugin The cleanest pattern mirrors the framework's own tests: drive the plugin through its real public API ( → → ) rather than unittesting internals. Tests look the same whether you're writing a plugin or consuming one. Packaging + publishing Ship your plugin as a normal Python package: and list in a host app's — that's the whole distribution story. No entry points or registration dance. Lifecycle Startup failures abort the ASGI app immediately — a plugin that can't reach its database should raise, and Pyxle propagates it so Starlette refuses to serve traffic. Shutdown failures are logged but don't abort teardown of the remaining plugins. Consuming services from your app Option 1: pluginprovided import helpers (preferred) Official plugins expose a typed helper function that reaches into the registry for you: This is the closest Pyxle gets to Django's pattern. Thirdparty plugins should ship similar helpers — it's one extra line of code for a much nicer consumer experience. Option 2: generic shortcut For services that don't have a typed helper (or adhoc access to the registry): raises when the name isn't registered and no default is provided. Error messages list every registered name, so typos are obvious at first request rather than silently returning . Option 3: long form via the request Useful inside middleware that already has the and wants to stay contextpure (no modulelevel state reads): When to use which App loaders and actions → option 1 (or 2 if no helper exists). Short and typed. Middleware, route hooks, requestscoped helpers → option 3. Avoids the modulelevel state read, which makes the middleware easier to test in isolation. CLI scripts or tests calling plugin code outside an ASGI context → install the context manually with and then any of the three forms work. Middleware contribution A plugin can declare ASGI middleware it wants appended to the host app's stack: Each entry is : Import string — either (Starletteconvention form) or (plain dotted form). Both resolve the same way. Options — a dict of keyword arguments passed through as , matching the shape of . Stack position Plugincontributed middleware sits between the host app's (from ) and the Vite proxy. In order from outermost to innermost the stack looks like: A plugin that adds requestid middleware therefore sees requests after CSRF has validated them but before the Pyxle router dispatches, which is usually what you want. Plugins that genuinely need to run before CSRF (e.g. a plugin handling webhooks with a different validation scheme) should register an exempt path via rather than trying to reorder middleware. When to use middleware vs. route hooks Middleware — crosscutting request transforms (request IDs, ratelimit headers, metrics). Runs on every request. Route hooks — perroute decoration (auth check on a subset of routes). Declared in by host apps, not plugins today. A plugin like deliberately doesn't install middleware — it ships a service the host app consumes inside its own loaders. That way apps stay in control of which routes require auth, and the plugin doesn't gate traffic the app owner didn't opt into. Service naming conventions Namespace Owner Each official plugin gets its own prefix. Reserved for the host app's own adhoc registrations. Plugins should document every name they register so consumers know what to ask for. Error handling Condition Exception Bad config entry (missing name, nonlist, etc.) at load time Module can't be imported at startup Attribute is missing / wrong type at startup raises wrapping the original — ASGI startup aborts misses at request time Troubleshooting The package isn't installed, the module path is wrong, or the plugin author forgot to create . Checklist: 1. — is the package actually installed in this interpreter? 2. — does the module import cleanly outside the devserver? 3. If the package uses a nonstandard module layout, set explicitly on the config entry: The package exports a attribute that isn't a subclass. Either the plugin author made a typo or you're pointing at the wrong attribute. Override on the config entry if the plugin uses a nonstandard export name: The listed available names are the full registry snapshot. Common causes: Typo. Compare letterbyletter with the available list. Wrong order. requires before it in the config list. Plugin not installed. The helper import succeeded but the plugin isn't in . Plugin startup raises Look at the wrapped — that's the plugin's real failure (DB not reachable, API key invalid, schema drift, etc). Fix that; the layer is just framing. raises "No active plugin context" in a test You're calling the helper outside an ASGI request. In tests, install a context manually: Changing doesn't take effect Pyxle resolves the plugins list at devserver startup, not perrequest. Restart (or your production server) after editing the plugins list. Limitations (Phase A) Plugins cannot contribute their own pages yet. To ship a default signin page today, the plugin author provides the source

searchIndex.title: Environment Variables
searchIndex.description: Pyxle loads environment variables from `.env` files and supports special prefixes for configuration overrides and client-side injection.
searchIndex.searchText: Environment Variables Pyxle loads environment variables from files and supports special prefixes for configuration overrides and clientside injection. files Create files in your project root: Load order Files are loaded in order of increasing precedence. Later files override earlier ones: 1. base defaults 2. or modespecific 3. local overrides (gitignore this) 4. or local mode overrides The mode is when running and when running or . The shell always wins Variables already set in your shell environment are never overwritten by files. This means CI/CD environment variables and Docker env take precedence. Syntax Doublequoted values support escape sequences: , , , , . prefix Variables starting with are injected into clientside JavaScript at build time: Access them in your JSX via : The value is substituted at build time into both the server (SSR) and client bundles, so it's safe to render directly into initial HTML — the server and client agree, with no hydration mismatch. Serveronly variables (without the prefix) are never exposed to the client. They are only available via in your Python code. config overrides These environment variables override settings from : Variable Config field Example or Precedence From lowest to highest: 1. Defaults in Pyxle 2. 3. files 4. environment variables 5. CLI flags (, , etc.) Page cache backend These select where the serverside page cache stores rendered HTML. The default needs no configuration. Variable Meaning (default), , , or inmemory: max entries before LRU eviction (default ) inmemory: max total body bytes (default , 64 MiB) file backend: directory for cache files (required for ) redis backend: connection URL (falls back to ) The cache is active only for production serves (); never caches. The backend needs . Realtime broker These select the WebSocket pub/sub broker. The default inprocess broker needs no configuration; switch to Redis for crossworker delivery under . Variable Meaning (default, inprocess) or (crossworker) redis broker: connection URL (default ) redis broker: channel namespace (default ) The broker needs . SSR rendering These tune the serverside rendering worker pool. The number of SSR processes is set with (autosized by default); this variable tunes how many renders each of those processes runs concurrently. Variable Meaning Max renders one SSR worker process handles concurrently (default ). Because streaming renders are mostly idle (awaiting loaders / ), one worker interleaves many of them; raise this only for workloads dominated by slow, I/Obound loaders Using environment variables in loaders files are loaded by the Pyxle CLI at startup (//). Outside the CLI — e.g. a loader unittested in isolation, or — they are not autoloaded; set the variables in the process environment yourself or call first. recommendations Next steps Handle errors gracefully: Error Handling Secure your app: Security

searchIndex.title: Error Handling
searchIndex.description: Pyxle provides structured error handling through error exceptions, error boundaries, and not-found pages.
searchIndex.searchText: Error Handling Pyxle provides structured error handling through error exceptions, error boundaries, and notfound pages. LoaderError Raise from a function to trigger the nearest error boundary: LoaderError parameters Parameter Type Default Description (required) Error message shown in the error boundary HTTP status code for the response Additional context passed to the error boundary ActionError Raise from an function to return a structured error to the client: The client receives . ActionError parameters Parameter Type Default Description (required) Error message sent to the client HTTP status code Additional data in the error response Error boundaries () Create an file to catch errors from pages in the same directory and below: An error boundary is a React component that receives the error context as props: Error props The prop contains: Property Type Description The error message HTTP status code Exception class name Additional data (if provided via ) Boundary resolution When an error occurs, Pyxle walks up the directory tree from the page that failed until it finds an : throws 1. Check 2. Check 3. Use default error document Clientside errors The same is also a clientside React error boundary. The server renders the nearest when a loader or the initial render fails; once the page is interactive, that boundary keeps working in the browser. If a component throws while rerendering — after a state update, during a clientside navigation, or on a hydration fault — the boundary catches it and renders the nearest in place, instead of React unmounting the page to a blank screen. It receives an prop with the same keys on both sides (, , ), so one renders consistently whether the fault happened on the server or in the browser. The values differ for a clientcaught render fault: there is no HTTP status in the browser, so the client boundary always reports and = the JS error name, and never carries (which is serveronly). Keep tolerant of being on the client. The boundary is transparent until something throws, so it never affects hydration, and it resets on the next navigation. In the error overlay still surfaces the full stack on top; in production the boundary is the userfacing fallback. This catches render faults. An error thrown in an event handler or an (e.g. a failed ) is not a render error — handle those where they occur (a , or surfacing an from ). Notfound pages () Create a file to customise the 404 page: Like error boundaries, notfound pages follow directory scoping a in handles 404s within . vs a loader 404. fires only for a request whose path matches no route. A raised from a real route does not render — it renders the nearest with . Branch on the status inside if you want a 404specific message there: is a normal page, not an error boundary: it receives no prop, and it may declare its own loader (it gets that loader's props). Use when you need the context; use for an unmatchedroute landing page. Dev mode error overlay During development (), errors also appear in a browser overlay with: The error message and stack trace Breadcrumbs showing which stage failed (loader, renderer, hydration) File path and line number The overlay communicates via WebSocket and updates in real time as you fix errors. Next steps Add clientside components: Client Components Secure your application: Security

searchIndex.title: Testing
searchIndex.description: Pyxle apps are mostly plain Python — your `@server` loaders and `@action` handlers are ordinary `async` functions, and your business logic is whatever Python you write. That means you can test with th
searchIndex.searchText: Testing Pyxle apps are mostly plain Python — your loaders and handlers are ordinary functions, and your business logic is whatever Python you write. That means you can test with the tools you already know (, ), at three levels: the loader/action in isolation, the plain logic underneath it, and the whole route endtoend. A file is not an importable Python module — it carries a JSX component next to the Python and compiles into a build directory, so does not work. Use (below) to load a page's functions, or keep your logic in plain modules. Unittesting a loader compiles a page and returns its loader as a plain function. Call it with a request — for a loader that only reads its , a lightweight standin like is enough: If your loader reads request attributes (query params, headers, ), give the fake request just those attributes: Unittesting an action returns the whole compiled module, so you can reach any (or the loader) by name: exposes the compiled page's metadata, including , if you need it. Best practice: keep loaders thin The cleanest code to test is code that doesn't need a request at all. Put real business logic in plain Python modules and let the loader/action be a thin adapter: This keeps the bulk of your test suite fast and frameworkfree; use for the thin loader/action adapters. Endtoend tests To exercise a real route — routing, SSR, middleware, and actions together — run the app and drive it over HTTP with a client like [](https://www.pythonhttpx.org/). This needs Node.js, because Pyxle renders React on the server. Start the server in your test harness (for example, a fixture that runs as a subprocess after a , waits for , and tears it down afterwards). See Deployment for the production server, and Observability for the and endpoints a fixture can poll. On the roadmap A firstclass — spin the app up inprocess and drive pages and actions (with CSRF handled for you) without managing a subprocess — is on the roadmap. Until then, the patterns above cover unit and endtoend testing with standard tools. Next steps Server Actions — the contract Deployment — run a production build for endtoend tests Observability — health endpoints for test fixtures

searchIndex.title: Debugging
searchIndex.description: A Pyxle app has two runtimes: **Python** (your `@server` loaders, `@action` handlers, and the request pipeline) and **Node.js** (the SSR worker that renders your React components to HTML). Knowing whi
searchIndex.searchText: Debugging A Pyxle app has two runtimes: Python (your loaders, handlers, and the request pipeline) and Node.js (the SSR worker that renders your React components to HTML). Knowing which side an error comes from tells you where to look. This guide covers both. Read the error first In development, Pyxle surfaces errors in three places at once: The browser error overlay — a fullscreen panel with the message, the file and line, and a breadcrumb of what the framework was doing (loading, rendering, head evaluation). This is the fastest signal for a broken page. The terminal running — the same error, plus the Python traceback for loader/action failures. The browser devtools console — your serverside output is forwarded here during , prefixed , so you can watch server logs without leaving the page. also forwards records and frameworkinternal logs. In production () error responses are intentionally generic — no stack traces or file paths in the body. The full detail goes to the server log instead. Check your process manager's logs (e.g. ). Debugging a loader or action (the Python side) and functions are plain functions — debug them like any Python. Drop a breakpoint: Run in a terminal you can type into (not detached) and the prompt appears there when the loader runs. Or add logging — it shows in the terminal and the browser console in dev: To unittest a loader/action in isolation (no server, no browser), use the helpers. Two common Pythonside errors have targeted messages: is missing (e.g. without the plugin) — the error names the attribute and the plugin that provides it. Add the plugin to . A loader raising triggers the nearest boundary; see Error Handling. Debugging rendering (the Node/SSR side) Your React component runs on the server during SSR before it ever runs in the browser. Errors that mention rendering come from the Node worker: (or , , …) — the component touched a browser global at render scope. These don't exist during SSR. Move the code into a or an event handler, or wrap the subtree in . The dev error names your file and the remedy — see Client Components. — a dependency resolved to a CommonJSonly build. Pyxle resolves dependencies ESMfirst; a package that is genuinely CommonJSonly reports an actionable error naming the module. See Thirdparty packages → CommonJS packages and SSR. inside a component prints to the terminal during the server render (and to the browser console after hydration) — so a value that logs twice is telling you it ran on both the server and the client. To step through the SSR worker itself, run it under the Node inspector by setting : then open . This is rarely needed for app code — it's for diagnosing the render transport itself. Reading the compiled output Pyxle compiles each into a Python module and a JavaScript module under the build directory ( in dev, for a production build). Reading them demystifies "where did my code go": — the Python half: your / functions with the runtime imports the compiler injected at the top (, etc.). — the JavaScript half: your component, ready for Vite. The line numbers in a Python traceback point at the compiled ; the compiler preserves your code verbatim below the injected header, so mapping back to your is a small, constant offset. The build directory is disposable — delete it and the next / regenerates it. Static checks Two commands catch problems before you run the page: — validates syntax, Python semantics, and JSX. Note that a green check does not prove a page renders: a component reading a loader key that doesn't exist is a runtime error, not a static one. — runs TypeScript over your compiled JSX when you've opted into TypeScript (see the TypeScript guide). Common symptoms Symptom Likely cause Where to look Blank page, overlay shows a Python traceback Loader/action raised terminal; add a Browser global at render scope The named ; use / Missing plugin in Component data is Loader key ≠ component prop Compare the loader's return dict to the component's usage Change didn't take effect Editing built output, or a stale build Edit the source, not Works in dev, fails in prod Debugonly behavior / missing env Server log (prod hides detail from the response) Next steps Error Handling — , error boundaries, 404s Testing — unittest loaders and actions Client Components — browseronly code and

searchIndex.description: Pyxle provides built-in React components and hooks importable from `pyxle/client`.
searchIndex.searchText: Client Components Pyxle provides builtin React components and hooks importable from . Manages document elements. See Head Management for full details. Renders nothing in the DOM. During SSR, its children are extracted and merged into the document head. Loads external scripts with configurable loading strategies: Props Prop Type Default Description (required for external) Script URL Loading strategy Add attribute Add attribute Load as Add the attribute (legacybrowser fallback) attribute Subresource Integrity (SRI) hash attribute Called when script loads Called on load failure; receives the Inline script source, used when is omitted Inline scripts (, no ) honour only ; the other props apply to external () scripts. See the reference for the full behaviour. Loading strategies Strategy When it loads In before hydration (blocking) After hydration completes (default) During browser idle time An optimized with lazy loading, layoutshift prevention, a blur placeholder, and — with a — responsive : loads the image eagerly with (use it for the LCP image); everything else lazyloads. Pass a (a CDN or build plugin) for responsive images, or to cover a positioned parent. See the Build Optimization guide for the full prop list and CDN/plugin integration, and the reference. Renders children only on the client, after hydration. Useful for components that depend on browser APIs: Props Prop Type Default Description Content to render on the client Shown during SSR and before hydration This prevents hydration mismatches for components that render differently on server vs client. Progressiveenhancement form component for calling server actions. See Server Actions for full details. Hook for calling server actions programmatically. See Server Actions for full details. Clientside navigation link. Prevents full page reloads: Programmatic clientside navigation: Prefetch a page's data and assets before navigation: Next steps Protect your app: Security Deploy to production: Deployment

searchIndex.title: Security
searchIndex.description: Pyxle includes CSRF protection, CORS support, and HEAD element sanitisation out of the box.
searchIndex.searchText: Security Pyxle includes CSRF protection, CORS support, and HEAD element sanitisation out of the box. CSRF protection CSRF (CrossSite Request Forgery) protection is enabled by default using a doublesubmit cookie pattern. How it works 1. Pyxle sets a CSRF cookie on every response. The default name is namespaced by the app's bind port — for an app serving on port 8000 2. Statechanging requests (, , , ) must echo the cookie value back, either in the header or — for formencoded submissions, such as a post before hydration — in a hidden form field 3. The framework validates that the submitted token matches the cookie using constanttime comparison 4. If they do not match, a response is returned The component and hook handle this automatically. Why the cookie name carries the port Browsers scope cookies to a host, not a host+port. With a fixed cookie name, two Pyxle apps on the same host — say two dev servers on and — would keep overwriting each other's token, and every action in the other app would fail with . Namespacing the default name with the bind port (read from the ASGI connection scope) gives each app its own cookie. The bind port is stable per app in development and behind a production reverse proxy, where the public port (443) differs from the bind port but never varies per request. Set to pin an explicit name instead — do this if you serve the same app from multiple ports and want one shared token, or if your production setup prerenders pages with and then serves them on a different port than declares (the prebaked pages embed the cookie name). The same applies if you run the ASGI app over a unix socket (e.g. raw ): with no bind port to namespace by, pin explicitly so the middleware and rendered pages always agree. Upgrading from an older Pyxle: a leftover cookie is simply ignored — the token reissues under the namespaced name on the next page load. No action needed. Form bodies and uploads Pyxle never buffers an unbounded request body to find : bodies are buffered up to 1 MiB; a larger body without the header token is rejected with . bodies are streamparsed only until the field is found — file parts after the token are passed through to your handler untouched, never buffered by the CSRF layer. renders the hidden token field first, so an upload of any size works out of the box. The scan is capped at 1 MiB of pretoken data; if the field hasn't appeared by then (or isn't present at all), the request is rejected with and a message telling you to send the token in the header or move before the file fields. Configuration Option Type Default Description Enable/disable CSRF auto: Cookie name; unset → namespaced by the bind port (see above) Header name Set flag (enable in production with HTTPS) attribute (, , or ) Paths exempt from CSRF checks; an entry matches its exact path and anything beneath it at a boundary ( does not exempt ) — see matching details Disabling CSRF Or with the object form: Token validation for custom fetch calls If you make requests outside of or , read the CSRF token from the cookie and include it as a header. Don't hardcode the cookie name — the default is portnamespaced, so resolve it from the global Pyxle injects into every page: A customised is exposed the same way, as (falling back to ). The globals always carry the effective names — including the auto portnamespaced cookie name, which client code cannot derive itself behind a reverse proxy. Signed cookies and tokens and (importable from ) attach a tamperproof signature to a string and verify it later — the building block for a signed session id, a "remember me" cookie, or a stateless link such as an unsubscribe or passwordreset URL. This is signing, not encryption: the value stays readable; a valid signature only proves it was produced by a holder of the secret. The signature is a full HMACSHA256 digest, compared in constant time. The secret is read from the environment variable, or passed explicitly as . Signing is meaningless without a secret, so a missing one raises rather than returning an unprotected value — it fails closed. Import the exception from (it is not reexported from the toplevel package): Use to namespace signatures by purpose, so a token signed for one flow can't be replayed in another even under the same secret: returns on any signature or format failure. Because a validlysigned empty string is itself falsy, test the result with , not truthiness: CORS configuration Configure CrossOrigin Resource Sharing for API access from other domains: Option Type Default Description Allowed origins (CORS is disabled if empty) Allowed methods Allowed request headers Allow credentials (cookies, auth headers) Preflight cache duration in seconds CORS is disabled by default (no configured). It is only active when you explicitly list allowed origins. Head element sanitisation Dynamic head content (whether from a JSX block or the lowerlevel variable) is automatically sanitised to prevent XSS: Tag allowlist: only , , , , and may appear; any other tag (, , , …) is dropped Attribute escaping: elements are parsed and rebuilt with every attribute value HTMLescaped, so a quote in dynamic data cannot break out of its attribute and inject markup Title text escaping: and inside elements are escaped to and , and markup injected after a closing is discarded Event handler stripping: , , , and all attributes are removed Dangerous URL neutralisation: , , and URLs in , , and attributes are emptied Meta refresh rejected: is dropped as an openredirect vector The text content of inline / head elements is trusted author code and is not sanitised. Never interpolate usersupplied data into inline script or style content. This sanitisation is applied to all head elements from every source — layout blocks, page blocks, and the legacy Python variable. Best practice React already escapes text content by default when you interpolate into JSX, so the component inherits XSS protection automatically: React escapes in the text node, and Pyxle's head sanitiser provides a second layer of defense. You don't need for JSXdriven head content. For the lowerlevel Python variable with usersupplied data, escape explicitly: Security headers Pyxle automatically sets a baseline of security headers on every response, in both dev and production — you don't need to add these at your proxy: allows your own pages to frame each other (e.g. a preview iframe) while blocking thirdparty framing. If you need stricter framing, set at your reverse proxy — a proxy header overrides the app's. Two headers are not set for you, because a safe value depends on your app: — a good policy is appspecific (which script/style/img/connect sources you allow), and a wrong one silently breaks your pages. Add it at your reverse proxy once you've settled your sources. A strict starting point is . (HSTS) — belongs at the TLSterminating layer (your proxy or CDN), since it only makes sense once you're serving HTTPS. Example: . Example Nginx addition for the two headers Pyxle leaves to you: Rate limiting Pyxle ships a builtin tokenbucket rate limiter, off by default. Enable it from to cap how fast a single client can hit the app and blunt bruteforce and scraping abuse: A client may burst up to and then sustains per second; overlimit requests get with a header. The store is inmemory and perprocess, so for a single shared limit across or hosts, ratelimit at your reverse proxy. Full details: middleware guide and configuration reference. Environment variable safety Variables without the prefix are serveronly and never appear in client bundles Loader and action return values are serialised to JSON and sent to the client never include secrets in return values Use for secrets and add it to Reporting a vulnerability Found a security issue in Pyxle itself? Please report it privately — don't open a public issue. Use a GitHub private advisory or email security@pyxle.dev. You'll get an acknowledgement within 72 hours. See SECURITY.md for the full policy and scope. N
