searchIndex.title: Observability
searchIndex.description: Pyxle gives every request a **correlation id** and measures how long it takes — on by default, with no setup. Both are cheap enough (a `uuid4` and two `perf_counter` reads, no I/O) to run on every req
searchIndex.searchText: Observability Pyxle gives every request a correlation id and measures how long it takes — on by default, with no setup. Both are cheap enough (a and two reads, no I/O) to run on every request in production. Heavier, exporterstyle observability — structured logs, a Prometheus metrics endpoint, OpenTelemetry tracing — is configured under the same [](../reference/configuration.md#observability) block and stays off until you opt in. Request IDs Every request is assigned a correlation id and the response carries it as the header: The same id is available to your server code on , so you can log it alongside your own messages and tie a userreported error back to a single request: A helper is also exported for code that may run before is populated: Trusting an upstream id By default Pyxle generates a fresh id and ignores any the client sent — echoing a clientsupplied value into your logs is a loginjection and spoofing vector. When Pyxle runs behind a trusted reverse proxy or gateway that already stamps a request id, opt in so the id flows through end to end: Even then, an incoming id is only honoured if it is wellformed (, at most 128 characters); anything else is replaced with a generated id. Custom header name Request timing With enabled (the default), Pyxle records each request's wallclock duration. The measurement is taken at the outermost layer, so it reflects the full time the framework spent on the request (to the point the response starts). This timing feeds the metrics and structuredlogging features; it is the foundation the rest of this guide builds on. Metrics Pyxle keeps an inprocess registry of request, SSRrender, loader, and durations (as fixedbucket histograms) plus pagecache hit/stale/miss counts. Recording is always on and cheap — a handful of integer adds per request, no perrequest allocation, bounded memory. Exposing those metrics is optin (the endpoint reveals internal state). Turn on a Prometheus endpoint: The real output prepends a line before each (elided above for brevity), and is the OS process id of the emitting worker. Guard it with a bearer token, and serve it on a custom path, if you like: With a token set, the endpoint requires (compared in constant time). The pagecache metrics require the serverside page cache, which runs under . Multiworker note. Under each worker process keeps its own registry, so the endpoint reports that worker's numbers — every series carries a label. Aggregate across workers at your Prometheus scraper (e.g. ). A shared crossworker registry is planned for a later release. Health probes Pyxle serves two probes for orchestrators (Kubernetes, load balancers): — liveness. Always while the process is up; the body reports , , and a flag. — readiness. once the server has warmed up and every dependency check passes, otherwise . The body lists each check: The dependency checks are fast attribute reads (never a network roundtrip), so the probe stays cheap. Today verifies the SSR worker pool has at least one live worker; point your readiness probe at it so traffic is only routed to a worker that can actually render. Structured logging Turn on a structured access log — one line per request, carrying the method, path, status, duration, and the request's correlation id: Set to (the default) for a humanreadable line instead, and to tune verbosity. The correlation id is bound into a context variable for the duration of the request, so your own log calls within a loader or action carry the same automatically when you log through the logger or your own logger configured the same way. Structured logging works with no extra dependency (a stdlib JSON/console formatter). Installing the optional extra adds structlog for richer rendering: It's off by default so it doesn't disrupt an existing log pipeline. OpenTelemetry tracing For distributed tracing, Pyxle emits OpenTelemetry spans for each request and child spans for the SSR render, loaders, and calls — so a slow page shows up as a trace you can drill into. It's the heaviest integration, so it's a separate optional extra and fully off by default. Install it and turn it on: The exporter endpoint is read from the standard environment variable (so it works with any OTLPcompatible collector). Sampling defaults to 5% so tracing can't swamp a busy server. When the extra isn't installed, spans are a noop with zero perrequest cost (a single boolean check) — but enabling without the extra installed fails loudly at startup rather than silently dropping traces. Tracing is kept entirely optional: a base Pyxle install pulls in no OpenTelemetry packages. Dev dashboard Run to print a live observability panel to the terminal every few seconds — a zerodependency, ataglance view of what your dev server is doing: It reads the same inprocess metrics described above, so it costs nothing on the request path, and it's devonly — there's no equivalent in , where you'd scrape the metrics endpoint instead. Turning it off Requestid and timing are individually toggleable, and a bare disables both: See the configuration reference for every key and default.

searchIndex.title: Deployment
searchIndex.description: Deploy a Pyxle application with `pyxle build` to compile assets and `pyxle serve` to run in production.
searchIndex.searchText: Deployment Deploy a Pyxle application with to compile assets and to run in production. Build for production This: 1. Compiles all files into Python and JSX modules 2. Runs a Vite production build — bundling JS and compiling every imported stylesheet (plain CSS, CSS Modules, and Tailwind v4 via the plugin when enabled) into contenthashed assets 3. Outputs production artifacts to the directory Build options Serve in production This starts a production Starlette server without Vite (static assets are served directly): In production mode () the server also compresses responses larger than 500 bytes with gzip automatically — no reverseproxy configuration needed for that. The gzip middleware is streamingaware (it flushes the compressor per chunk), so streaming SSR still delivers the shell first behind gzip in production rather than buffering the whole response. Serve options Flag Default Description Bind address Port number / Server worker processes — one per CPU core (multicore) Persistent Node.js SSR processes, per server worker (/ = size to CPU cores, capped at 4). defaults to auto; pass a number to pin it Directory with production artifacts Skip running build first Serve static assets directly (disable when a CDN hosts them) Path to an alternate config file Build + serve in one step By default, runs first. Skip this with : Multicore (worker processes) By default runs a single async server process, which uses one CPU core. To use every core on a multicore server, run one server worker process per core with (requires Pyxle 0.4.3+): autodetects the core count, so you don't have to hardcode it for the target host. Each worker is an independent server process with its own SSR worker pool, all sharing one listening socket — incoming connections are balanced across them with no load balancer and no shared state to configure. Throughput on CPUbound endpoints scales nearlinearly with the worker count. builds the project once before the workers start, so the build is never duplicated. Combine with only when already exists. Workers reconstruct their configuration from environment variables exported by the parent process — these are internal; don't set them yourself. SSR workers Serverside rendering runs in persistent Node.js processes that stay warm between requests. autosizes the pool by default — the pool grows to the machine's CPU count, capped at 4, per server worker — so you don't have to think about it for most deployments: applies per server worker, so the total number of Node.js render processes is . Passing (or omitting the flag) autosizes the pool to the machine's CPU count (capped at 4) per server worker. Concurrency within a worker Each SSR worker handles many renders concurrently, not one at a time. This matters most for streaming SSR: a streaming render spends almost all of its wallclock time idle, awaiting loader promises and boundaries. The worker interleaves those idle windows, so overlapping requests to a streaming page all start rendering immediately instead of queueing behind each other. Perrequest state (pathname, CSRF token, tags, styles) is isolated per render, so interleaving never mixes one visitor's page into another's. The inworker concurrency cap defaults to 16 inflight renders and is tunable with the environment variable — raise it only for a workload dominated by slow, I/Obound loaders where renders sit idle waiting on the network. Because the cap governs idle concurrency, adding SSR processes () is what adds CPUparallel render throughput. Sizing guidance Workload Suggestion APIheavy, little SSR SSRheavy / streaming pages Defaults are fine — the autosized pool renders concurrently. Raise only if CPUbound renders queue Small VPS (1–2 cores) Defaults (, auto SSR pool) are fine Memoryconstrained Each worker is a full process — reduce before reducing Perworker state (multiworker caveats) Each worker is a separate process with no shared memory, which is what makes multicore serving trivial to operate — there's nothing to coordinate. The tradeoff is that any inprocess state is perworker: Feature Perworker behaviour For crossworker behaviour Page cache Each worker has its own inmemory cache Use the Redis backend () — shared across workers and hosts WebSocket pub/sub Messages reach only clients on the same worker Use the Redis broker () — relays channels across workers and hosts Metrics (optin via ) reports that worker's numbers (with a label) Aggregate at the Prometheus scraper Background tasks queue is perworker Use a real job queue (Celery / ARQ / Dramatiq) This is by design: Pyxle keeps the default path stateless so workers fork cleanly and scale linearly. The escape hatch is always a shared backend (Redis for cache/pubsub) or an external service (a job queue, a Prometheus scraper), not a shared inprocess resource — so the same code runs at one worker or fifty. Why not a single shared SSR pool across workers? A shared Node pool over a Unix socket would save some memory but add a coordination point and a single contention bottleneck, undermining the sharenothing model. Perworker pools stay simple and isolated — a crashed render can't affect another worker. Size with instead. Rolling deploys & graceful restart For zerodowntime deploys, don't restart Pyxle in place — drain and replace: 1. Behind a load balancer / reverse proxy: start a new instance (new ) on a second port, wait for to return (see Observability → health probes), shift traffic to it, then stop the old one. This gives true zerodowntime. 2. Single host, process manager: run under systemd (or supervisor). A cleanly stops the old workers (uvicorn handles , finishing inflight requests) and starts new ones — a subsecond blip, fine for most apps. Point your readiness probe at so the proxy only routes once a worker is actually ready. Bluegreen (option 1) is the recommended zerodowntime path — and it sidesteps the question of preloading entirely. Bluegreen gotcha — pin the CSRF cookie name and share the secret. The two instances run on different ports, and by default the CSRF cookie is named (perport, so multiple apps on one host don't collide). In a bluegreen cutover that backfires: a token the blue instance issued as is rejected by green on , so midshift POSTs . Pin an explicit, shared name so both instances agree: Both instances must also share the same — a token signed by one is only valid on the other if the signing key matches. A note on preloading. imports each worker's app after forking (uvicorn's model), so there's no copyonwrite sharing of readonly state between workers. In practice this rarely matters — Python's perprocess memory is dominated by the interpreter and the SSR Node pool, not your page modules. If you specifically need importoncethenfork, run the app under a process manager that supports it (e.g. gunicorn with ), pointing at the factory with set to your project directory — gunicorn then owns worker lifecycle and graceful rolling restarts. This is an advanced setup; the bluegreen approach above is simpler and gives true zerodowntime. Environment configuration Set production settings via environment variables: is required in production. It signs CSRF tokens and any signed cookies (sessions, unsubscribe links). refuses to start without it (unless you've set ). Generate a long random value once and keep it stable — store it in your process manager's environment or a secrets manager, never in the repo. Rotating it invalidates outstanding tokens and signed links. Or in a file: Variable Overrides Signing secret for CSRF tokens and signed cookies (required in production) Bind address Port number Debug mode (/ or /) Pages directory Static assets directory Build output directory CLI flags win over environment variables, which win over . Variables prefixed are exposed to client code — never put secrets in them. Reverse proxy setup In production, place Pyxle behind a reverse proxy (Nginx, Caddy, etc.) for TLS termination, load balancing, and static asset caching. Nginx ex

searchIndex.title: Editor setup
searchIndex.description: Pyxle Language Tools brings first-class IDE support to `.pyxl` files — syntax highlighting, diagnostics, completions, hover documentation, go-to-definition, and formatting. Everything works out of the
searchIndex.searchText: Editor setup Pyxle Language Tools brings firstclass IDE support to files — syntax highlighting, diagnostics, completions, hover documentation, gotodefinition, and formatting. Everything works out of the box with a single install. Beta release. The language toolkit is productionusable but still evolving. Report issues on GitHub. VS Code (recommended) 1. Install the extension Search for "Pyxle" in the VS Code Extensions panel, or install from the terminal: The extension is also available on the VS Code Marketplace. 2. Install the language server The VS Code extension is a thin client — the actual intelligence comes from the process. As of 0.7.0 it ships with the framework itself — if you've run , is already in your environment. (On older framework versions, installs it separately.) That's it. Open any file and you'll see the ✓ Pyxle indicator in the status bar when the language server connects. Features Syntax highlighting files get duallanguage highlighting — Python sections are highlighted as Python, JSX sections as JavaScript/React. The grammar correctly handles template literals, so code snippets inside backtick strings won't confuse the highlighter. Diagnostics Errors and warnings appear inline as you type: Python analysis — powered by pyflakes with 40+ rules. Catches undefined names, unused imports, syntax errors, unreachable code, and more. JSX analysis — powered by Babel. Catches syntax errors in your JSX code. Pyxlespecific rules — validates loaders (async, request parameter, return statement), functions, and component props, element format, and default export requirements. Completions Intelligent autocompletion for both sections: Python — powered by Jedi. Full module completions, function signatures, attribute access, import suggestions. JSX — powered by TypeScript's language service. React component props, DOM attributes, imported symbols, and local variables. Pyxlespecific — and decorator snippets, boilerplate, Pyxle component names (, , , , , , ), and property completions inferred from your loader's return statement. Hover documentation Hover over any symbol to see its type and documentation: Python symbols — function signatures, docstrings, and type info from Jedi. JSX symbols — type information from TypeScript. Pyxle decorators — hover or for a quick reference of the decorator contract. Pyxle components — hover , , , etc. for prop documentation. Gotodefinition Cmd+Click (macOS) or Ctrl+Click (Windows/Linux) to jump to definitions: Python symbols — jump to function definitions, class declarations, and imported modules via Jedi. JSX symbols — jump to component definitions, imported functions, and type declarations via TypeScript. Crosssection — click in JSX to jump to the key in your loader's return statement. Document symbols Open the Outline panel (Cmd+Shift+O) to see all symbols in the current file: loaders, actions, functions, classes, and JSX exports. Formatting Format files with sectionaware formatting: Python sections — formatted with ruff (or optionally black). JSX sections — formatted with prettier. Each section is formatted independently, preserving the file structure. Semantic highlighting Enhanced syntax coloring beyond the TextMate grammar — decorators, function parameters, builtin calls, and constants get distinct colors based on AST analysis. Configuration The extension exposes these settings (VS Code Settings → search "Pyxle"): Setting Default Description Command to launch the language server Arguments for the language server Python formatter (, , or ) JSX formatter ( or ) Enable pyflakes diagnostics Enable Babelbased JSX analysis Using a virtual environment If is installed in a virtual environment, point the extension to the full path: CLI tools The language toolkit also provides commandline tools for CI pipelines and scripting: Lint in CI Add to your CI pipeline to catch issues before merge: Other editors The language server speaks the standard Language Server Protocol and works with any LSPcompatible editor. Neovim Using nvimlspconfig: You'll also need to register the filetype: Sublime Text Using LSP: JetBrains IDEs JetBrains IDEs have limited LSP support. A dedicated plugin is planned for a future release. Troubleshooting "pyxlelangserver: command not found" The language server isn't on your PATH. Either: Install it (ships with the framework): — or standalone: Or set the full path in VS Code settings: No JSX completions JSX completions require Node.js and TypeScript installed in your project's . The language server spawns a TypeScript language service worker for JSX intelligence. No Python completions Python completions are powered by Jedi, which is installed automatically with . If completions are missing, verify the install: Status bar shows "✗ Pyxle" The language server failed to start. Check the Output panel in VS Code (View → Output → select "Pyxle Language Server") for error details. Formatting doesn't change anything Formatting requires the formatter binaries to be on your PATH: Python: (or ) JSX: (or install in your project) Architecture The language toolkit has a serverdriven architecture — all intelligence lives in the Python LSP server: Python analysis — Jedi runs inprocess for completions, hover, and gotodefinition. JSX analysis — An embedded TypeScript language service runs as a Node.js subprocess, communicating via NDJSON over stdin/stdout. Diagnostics — pyflakes for Python static analysis, Babel for JSX validation, plus Pyxlespecific rules. No virtual files — unlike some language tools that write temporary files to disk, Pyxle's language server keeps everything in memory. The VS Code extension is a thin LSP client (100 lines of TypeScript) that connects to the server and manages the status bar. All the intelligence is in the server, making it easy to support other editors. Source code: github.com/pyxledev/pyxlelangkit

searchIndex.title: TypeScript
searchIndex.description: Pyxle gives you typed editor support and an opt-in type-check gate **without** asking you to write TypeScript in your `.pyxl` files. The model is **consumption, not authoring**: your server code is ty
searchIndex.searchText: TypeScript Pyxle gives you typed editor support and an optin typecheck gate without asking you to write TypeScript in your files. The model is consumption, not authoring: your server code is typed Python, your client code is plain JSX, and Pyxle generates the TypeScript declarations and config that make the framework surface fully typed in your editor. What "TypeScript support" means in Pyxle today Three concrete things, all generated for you on every / : 1. A under , with strict mode and the right path aliases. 2. A full set of declarations for every hook and component, so imports resolve to real types. 3. The command, which runs over the generated client so you can gate CI on type errors. What it does not include is TypeScript syntax in the JSX block of a file. The compiler emits the client component as , and esbuild (used by both the SSR runtime and Vite) loads it with its JSX loader, not the TypeScript loader — so type annotations, , generics, and casts in a client block aren't transpiled. Pyxle flags them at compile time with a clear error pointing at your file, rather than letting them fail later in the bundler (see Limitations). In short: the client block is plain JSX that gets typechecked, never typeannotated. Serverside types (Python) The and halves of a file are ordinary Python — type them with normal Python hints. An body parameter can be annotated with a Pydantic model for automatic request validation, and generates an OpenAPI 3.1 document straight from those models. This is independent of the client TypeScript toolchain below. See Server actions → Validating request bodies. The generated client project Every build writes a selfcontained TypeScript project under . It is frameworkowned and regenerated each run — don't edit it. It contains the , the compiled pages, and a folder of declarations: Declaration Types the navigation runtime: , , , , , , (reexports the component types below) , , , , , , , , , , slot types , , , , , , , , Typed framework imports Because of those declarations, importing from gives you full completions, hover, and signature help: is generic in the data your action returns. For Pydantic validation errors, the invoker exposes a reactive map, and delivers field errors to its callback. These types describe the framework surface — they do not infer the shape of your own loader's (see Limitations). The generated The scaffolded config turns on strict mode and wires the import aliases Pyxle uses: Resolution is (not the legacy /) because your code is bundled by Vite + esbuild, not resolved by Node at runtime — and TypeScript 7.0 deprecates resolution and outright, so a current would reject them. work without : they resolve relative to the . Two honest notes on coverage: is not enabled, so your own page JSX — including how it calls the typed framework imports — isn't typechecked yet: skips the bodies of files unless is on. What the gate verifies today is the framework surface itself (the generated declarations, config, and path wiring) plus any TypeScript sources you add. Checking page JSX against the framework types (wrong props, a bad call) requires + React types and is planned — see The road to a fullytyped Pyxle. covers but not , so files under a source root currently fall outside the typecheck glob. compiles your files to and runs against the generated , surfacing any type errors against the framework declarations. It looks for in your local , then globally — TypeScript must actually be installed (locally is recommended: ); if it isn't, the command fails fast with that instruction rather than invoking anything. It's a useful CI gate for the generated client project and any TypeScript you add — see the honest scope note above for what it does and doesn't check today. See the CLI reference. Editor setup The pyxlelangkit language server runs a TypeScript language service over the JSX section of your files, so you get completions and hover from the generated declarations as you type. It needs Node and available in the project. See Editor setup. Limitations TypeScript support today is consumptiononly. The following are not yet available and are tracked for a later phase — they are roadmap items, not bugs: No TypeScript syntax in the client block. The compiler emits and the bundlers use a JSX loader, so , , generics, and casts aren't supported in a JSX block — keep the client half plain JSX. Pyxle's compiler flags TypeScript syntax there with a clear, sourcelocated error. No authoring. You can't write the client half as full /TypeScript yet. No perpage loadertype generation. Your loader's arrives on the client as an untyped object — there's no generated describing a specific loader's return shape. No OpenAPI → TypeScript client generation from your action models. No single barrel. Types are permodule plus ; the barrel itself resolves as plain JS via . These are tracked for a later phase. The strict scaffold and the framework set are delivered today; loadertype generation and authoring remain pending. For the full picture of where the type story is heading — authoring your client in TypeScript and forwarding Python types to the client — see The road to a fullytyped Pyxle. Next steps Where this is heading: The road to a fullytyped Pyxle Configure your editor: Editor setup Validate action inputs with types: Server actions CLI reference for : CLI
headings: What "TypeScript support" means in Pyxle today

searchIndex.title: The road to a fully-typed Pyxle
searchIndex.description: Pyxle is Python-first. That is a deliberate choice, not a limitation — but it raises an obvious question for anyone who has felt the pull of end-to-end types in a TypeScript stack: *if my server is Py
searchIndex.searchText: The road to a fullytyped Pyxle Status: roadmap. This page describes where Pyxle's type story is going, not a feature that ships today. For what's available now — typed editor support, generated declarations, and the gate — see the TypeScript guide. Nothing here changes the fact that JavaScript is fully firstclass today: every npm package works in a JSX block, and nothing is blocked. Pyxle is Pythonfirst. That is a deliberate choice, not a limitation — but it raises an obvious question for anyone who has felt the pull of endtoend types in a TypeScript stack: if my server is Python and my client is React, where do my types come from? This is our answer, and the direction we are building toward. The vision: one source of truth, typed across the boundary In a Pyxle app the shape of your data is defined once, in Python — a loader returns a Pydantic model, a dataclass, a , or an annotated ; an accepts a validated body. The endgame is that this shape flows to the client as a real type, so the your component receives is typed from the Python that produced it, and a mismatch is caught before the page ever renders. No handwritten client interfaces to keep in sync. No duplicated shapes drifting apart. Python stays the single source of truth, and the type crosses the Python↔React boundary with it. That is the thing a Pythonfirst fullstack framework is uniquely positioned to offer — and it's what "a fullytyped Pyxle" means. What's already true today This isn't a green field. The framework already understands the boundary the type bridge will cross: catches undefined names and semantic mistakes in your files before they run. generates a full set of declarations for the runtime and typechecks your client against them with — so the framework surface is already typed in your editor and gateable in CI. bodies validate against Pydantic models at runtime today, and emits an OpenAPI 3.1 schema straight from those models. The server already treats your Python types as the contract. The compiler extracts loader and action metadata at build time. The information the type bridge needs to reason about is already on the table. So the direction below isn't a rewrite — it's connecting foundations that are already in place. Where it's going Two pieces, in roughly this order: 1. Author your client in TypeScript. Write the JSX half of a file in TSX — annotations, , generics — with full editor intelligence. (Today the client block is plain JSX that gets typechecked but not typeannotated; this lifts that restriction.) 2. Forward Python types to the client. Derive a TypeScript type from a loader's or action's Python return/parameter type and thread it into the component's props, kept in sync automatically as your Python changes. This is the differentiator, and the harder half — it's where most of the design work lives. We're describing the direction, not committing to a specific mechanism yet. The right inference strategy, codegeneration approach, and editor integration are exactly what a dedicated design pass will settle — and we'd rather ship that properly than nail a halfanswer to a page. Principles we're holding to Python is the source of truth. Types are derived from your Python, never handduplicated on the client. TypeScript is optin, not a tax. JavaScript stays fully firstclass. A JS Pyxle app is a real, supported Pyxle app — today and after this lands. No lockin and no magic. Generated types are inspectable; nothing hides behavior — in keeping with Pyxle's "no magic" design principle. Honest tooling. A green typecheck will mean what it says. A taste of what it should feel like Illustrative — this is the target ergonomics, not current syntax. The point isn't the syntax — it's that was written once, in Python, and the client got it for free. Status and how to follow along This is planned, not scheduled — we're deliberately not attaching a version or date to it, because the design pass comes first and we won't precommit to a mechanism we haven't validated. What you can rely on in the meantime: JavaScript is fully supported, with no capability or package gaps. The typededitorsupport and features on the TypeScript guide are here today. When the type bridge lands, it will be additive — it won't break your JS app. If typed endtoend data is the thing that would make Pyxle a yes for you, this page is our commitment that it's where we're headed — and that we intend to do it right.

searchIndex.title: Pyxle for AI coding agents
searchIndex.description: Copilot, whatever you use — can read, write, debug, and ship real full-stack features with a tiny fraction of the context, files, tools, and languages that traditional frameworks demand. One file per
searchIndex.searchText: Pyxle for AI coding agents TL;DR. Pyxle is built so a coding agent — Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, whatever you use — can read, write, debug, and ship real fullstack features with a tiny fraction of the context, files, tools, and languages that traditional frameworks demand. One file per page, Python backend, React frontend, zero config, structured errors, no magic. If your workflow involves pairing with an AI agent, Pyxle is the framework most optimised for that pairing. This isn't a marketing claim about being "AIpowered" — Pyxle doesn't call any LLMs itself. It's a claim about developer experience: the framework is shaped in a way that makes agents dramatically more effective per token spent. The problem agents have with modern web frameworks When a coding agent builds a feature in a typical Next.js + FastAPI stack, it has to simultaneously hold in mind: A TypeScript frontend and a Python backend — two languages, two type systems, two ecosystems, two build tools. A UI page component in . An API route in (or a separate FastAPI endpoint in a different repo). A datafetching hook in . A CORS config connecting the two backends. A shared types file (or worse, diverging types on each side). A , a , a , a , a , and usually at least one Dockerfile. For the agent, every one of those is context. Context is tokens, tokens are money, and every additional file the agent has to open is another opportunity to get the mental model wrong. Multifile edits are where most AI agents struggle the most — not because they can't reason about code, but because keeping N files consistent with each other across edits is genuinely hard. Now watch what the same feature looks like in Pyxle. One page, one file, both languages Here's the entire "list users and delete them" feature in Pyxle: That's it. One file, one route (), one feature. The agent reads this file, understands the whole thing in a single pass, and can make coherent edits to the loader, the mutation, and the UI without opening anything else. Compare the file count and crossfile coordination burden: What Next.js + FastAPI Pyxle Files for one feature 46 1 Languages TypeScript, Python Python, JSX Typeshare mechanism Manual / codegen Native dict Build configs 34 1 HTTP hops per render 12 (page → API) 0 (inproc) CORS to configure Yes No Context per feature High Low Why this matters for an AI agent specifically 1. Python is the language LLMs know best Every major LLM has seen dramatically more Python code during training than any other language. When an agent writes Python, it is operating in its highestconfidence mode. When it writes TypeScript, it is operating at one tier below that. When it writes the interaction between two languages — a TypeScript fetch call hitting a Python FastAPI endpoint — errors compound across the boundary and the agent has to reason about the contract in both directions simultaneously. Pyxle keeps the agent in Python for everything datarelated, and only asks it to write JSX for the view layer. The agent spends its strongest muscle on its strongest language. 2. Python is the language the AI ecosystem lives in Every serious LLM SDK — the Anthropic SDK, OpenAI SDK, LangChain, LlamaIndex, instructor, pydantic AI, the Claude Agent SDK — is Pythonfirst. Most have JavaScript ports that are perpetually behind in features. If your app integrates an LLM, a Pyxle loader can just directly. No microservice, no separate Python worker, no HTTP hop, no API token shuttling between frontends and backends. One file, one async call, response as a React prop. An agent building this feature doesn't need to open three repos. 3. No magic — the code you see is the code that runs Pyxle's and decorators set one attribute each and return the function unchanged. There is no dependency injection container, no runtime monkeypatching, no metaclass inheritance, no proxy object intercepting attribute access. You can read in a few seconds — it's around 150 lines including docstrings. This is a massive advantage for agents because the agent's mental model of the code matches the actual execution. In frameworks with heavy runtime reflection (Django admin, Spring, Rails, NestJS), the agent's debugging loop can break when its inferred behaviour doesn't match what the framework actually does at runtime. With Pyxle, the agent can reason locally. The decorator does nothing special. The function runs when the router calls it. The dict you return becomes the prop on the React side. That's all. 4. Conventional file layout is the routing table There is no , no , no route decorators. If you have , the route is . The agent doesn't have to open a router file to learn what routes exist — it can and be done. For an agent, this means zero tokens spent reading routing configuration. The filesystem is the configuration. 5. gives agents structured, perfile errors Most framework linters or type checkers print errors as a wall of text and the agent has to parse them. Pyxle's command prints diagnostics with a rigid, machinefriendly shape: Each line is followed by the file path on an indented next line. An agent can split on newlines, extract the file, the line number, and the message, and use that to plan its next edit. This is the format AI agents love. It's exactly what a grepbased tool call wants to consume. The checker also: Runs every file every time — one bad file never aborts the scan, so the agent gets a complete picture of project health after every edit. Reports both Python and JSX errors in the same pass. Suppresses cascade noise — fix Python first, and the downstream JSX errors that came from the broken Python vanish automatically. The agent isn't chasing ghosts. Has an exit code — if clean, if errors. Shellfriendly for agent workflows that gate subsequent commands on check success. 6. Tiny CLI surface The CLI is small and singlepurpose: (plus for TypeScriptchecking compiled JSX — that's the whole surface.) There is no , no , no . Every command has a clear, singlepurpose behaviour, and the agent doesn't have to search the docs to figure out which command does what. 7. Async by default, enforced at compile time AI SDKs are async. The Anthropic SDK, the OpenAI SDK, httpx, most modern database drivers — they all expect async. Pyxle enforces async at the parser level. is a compile error. is a compile error. The agent cannot accidentally write a blocking handler that would stall the event loop when calling an LLM. The framework protects against an entire class of mistake before the agent makes it. 8. The dev overlay shows agentfriendly errors in real time When a loader raises at request time, the dev overlay shows: The route that failed The exception type (e.g. ) The exception message verbatim A breadcrumb list describing which stage failed (loader, head, renderer, hydration) An agent with access to the browser (via a screenshot tool or a browser MCP) can read the overlay directly and understand the failure without needing to a log file. Most frameworks either print errors to the server log (invisible to the agent unless it knows to check), or render a generic 500 page with no useful detail. 9. The architecture is fully documented This is the part most frameworks don't bother with, and it's the part that matters most for an agent trying to make nontrivial changes. The architecture handbook is an 11document deepdive covering every subsystem of the framework: the parser, the compiler, the dev server, the SSR pipeline, the routing, the build, the runtime, and the CLI. It's written so that an agent can read one doc to answer one question, instead of inferring framework behaviour from source code. Specifically: The parser explains how files are split into Python and JSX. An agent debugging a parse issue can read this once and know exactly what's happening. Routing has a complete table mapping filenames to URLs (dynamic segments, catchalls, layouts, error boundaries). The runtime shows the full 83 lines of inline. There is no hidden decorator magi

searchIndex.title: AI accessibility — your app in Markdown
searchIndex.description: Every page of a Pyxle app can serve a clean **Markdown** rendition of itself — so AI assistants and coding agents (Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, Copilot, Perplexity) read your app as text instead of scrapi
searchIndex.searchText: AI accessibility — your app in Markdown Every page of a Pyxle app can serve a clean Markdown rendition of itself — so AI assistants and coding agents (Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, Copilot, Perplexity) read your app as text instead of scraping HTML. Turn it on with one flag; decide where the Markdown comes from with a few conventions in your project. That single line gives you: Perpage Markdown at each URL with appended — → . Content negotiation — the same URL returns Markdown to any request whose header asks for , negotiated with proper RFC 9110 qvalues. Browsers never send it, so humans are unaffected. An index — the llms.txt convention: a Markdown map of your site. Discovery headers on every response — and — so an agent finds the index without parsing HTML. This very page proves it: append to its URL to read the Markdown you're looking at. The mental model There are two moving parts, and they're cleanly separated: 1. Routing is the framework's job. When is enabled, Pyxle registers a route for every page, wires up negotiation, serves , and adds the discovery headers. You don't configure any of that. 2. Content is your job — expressed as files, not config. Where a page's Markdown comes from is resolved from your project on each request. The config block is just an on/off switch (plus one optin fallback); everything substantive lives in files and handlers next to your routes. The feature is off by default and adds nothing to the normal page render path — the routes are separate and only run for (or ) requests. Enabling it In : Key Default What it does Turns the whole feature on. is shorthand for . A lastresort fallback: convert a page's rendered HTML to Markdown when no authored source exists. Off by default because it's lossy — see autoConvert. That is the entire configuration surface. See Configuration → AI accessibility. How a page's Markdown is resolved For any request (or an request to the page), Pyxle walks this ladder and uses the first source that returns text: Source Scope Best for 1 Colocated file one page static, handwritten pages 2 in the page's own module one page (a catchall covers its subtree) pages that already load their content 3 in the nearest ancestor a route subtree ( = appwide) one handler for many pages 4 (only if enabled) any page a rough fallback when nothing else exists 5 Redirect → — so a guessed URL never 404s Whatever a rung returns is then passed through the optional [](#framingeverypagewrapmarkdown) hook before it's sent. 1. A colocated file Drop a Markdown file next to the page. Simplest possible option — no code: Best for static, contentheavy pages you'd rather write by hand than generate — a landing page, a manifesto, a pricing page. 2. A pagelocal handler Add a function to a page's Python (server) section. It receives a [](#themarkdowncontextctx) and returns a Markdown string — or to defer to the next rung: Because a catchall page () is a single page that handles every subpath, its already covers the whole subtree — read to know which page was asked for. 3. A directory handler (covers a route subtree) To serve many pages under a directory with one handler, put a in an at that directory. Resolution walks from the page's own directory up to , nearest ancestor first — exactly like , , and : Returning declines and defers to the next ancestor (and ultimately to /redirect). So a handler can answer the slugs it knows and let everything else fall through — handlers compose down the tree. is also the intended home for any future perdirectory AI hooks (it already hosts [](#thellmstxtindex) and [](#framingeverypagewrapmarkdown) at the root). autoConvert (the lossy fallback) If nothing above resolves and you've set , Pyxle renders the page and converts its HTML to Markdown with a small, dependencyfree converter. It's off by default and deliberately besteffort: headings, paragraphs, lists, links, emphasis, and code survive; layout chrome, tables, and rich components may not. Treat it as "something is better than a redirect" — prefer an authored or a handler for anything you care about. Converted pages keep agents on the Markdown channel: internal links to other pages are rewritten to their renditions — becomes , with query strings and fragments preserved ( → ). External URLs, / links, routes, asset paths with a file extension, and links already ending in are left untouched. See [](#ctxrenderhtmlpostprocessingtherenderedpage) for the same behavior in your own handlers. The redirect fallback With the feature on but no Markdown source and off, returns a redirect to . An agent that guesses a URL lands on the real page instead of a 404. The () Every and handler receives a single argument — a . It's a small, readonly object with everything you need to produce a page's Markdown: Member Type Description The incoming request. Use it for route params, query string, headers, and the body. The canonical page path, always without — e.g. , or for the home page. Use this, not . Runs only the page's loader and returns its data — the dict the page would receive — skipping the render. The cheap path when you just want the loaded data. Returns for a page with no loader. Renders the original page — running its loader and full SSR — and returns the body HTML (the component output, without the document shell). Lazy: nothing renders unless you call it. — the request object A standard Starlette [](https://www.starlette.io/requests/). The members you'll actually reach for: — the matched route parameters. For , is . This is how one handler serves many pages. — the query string (), a multidict. — request headers. / — the request body, if any. vs — an important distinction Use . It is always the canonical page path with no suffix, whether the request arrived as or as with . In contrast, carries the raw request path — which includes on a request and omits it on an negotiated one. Reading means your handler behaves identically on both entry points. — postprocessing the rendered page When you want to derive Markdown from what the page actually renders — rather than from source data — call . It runs the page's loader and server render and hands you the body HTML, which you can transform: rewrites internal page links to their renditions by default (exactly like autoConvert); pass to keep every verbatim. It's lazy and potentially expensive (a full SSR pass), so only call it when you need it. When you want the page's data rather than its rendered HTML, reach for instead — it's much cheaper. — the loader's data, without the render Often you don't want rendered HTML at all — you want the same data the page loads, to format as Markdown yourself. runs just the page's loader and returns its result (the dict the page would receive as ), skipping SSR entirely: This is the cheap path — a loader call, not a full render — and it reuses the exact dataloading your page already does. A page with no loader returns . Handler contract Sync or async — both work. An async handler is awaited. Return to serve that Markdown. Return to decline and fall through to the next rung. Returning anything else raises a (surfaced in logs); the request degrades gracefully to a redirect, and an negotiated request falls back to HTML. Framing every page () To add a consistent header/footer to every response — agent instructions, navigation hints, a canonicalURL banner — define a function in the root . Pyxle calls it with the and the alreadyresolved Markdown, and serves whatever string it returns: Because it runs on Markdown from every source (colocated files, handlers, ), the framing is defined once and applied everywhere — and it always receives the final resolved Markdown, so output arrives with its internal links already rewritten to . Return to leave the Markdown untouched. And because it's applied at serve time — not baked into your source — your corpus and the raw files stay clean. The index [](https://llmstxt.org/) is a Markdown map of your site that agents (and humans) can read to discover wha

searchIndex.title: Migrating from .pyx to .pyxl
searchIndex.description: Pyxle's file extension has changed from `.pyx` to `.pyxl`. This guide explains why and how to update your project.
searchIndex.searchText: Migrating from to Pyxle's file extension has changed from to . This guide explains why and how to update your project. Why the change? The extension is already used by Cython, a wellestablished Python compiler. This collision caused real problems: GitHub misclassification — GitHub's linguist identifies files as Cython, causing repository language stats to show "Cython" instead of "Pyxle." IDE conflicts — VS Code, JetBrains, and other editors default to Cython syntax highlighting when opening files, requiring manual overrides. Search confusion — Searching for ".pyx files" returns Cython documentation and tutorials, making Pyxle harder to discover. Tooling friction — Linters, formatters, and CI tools with Cython support may interfere with Pyxle files. The new extension is unique, maps clearly to the "Pyxle" name, and eliminates all of these issues. What changed? Only the file extension. The file format, syntax, and behavior are completely identical: Before After Everything inside the files — Python server blocks, JSX client code, loaders, mutations — works exactly the same. How to migrate 1. Rename all files to 2. Update imports between pages If any of your files import from other pages, update the extension in the import path: 3. Update the VS Code extension If you're using the Pyxle VS Code extension (), update to the latest version. The new version registers as the file extension for syntax highlighting, diagnostics, and formatting. 4. Verify Run to confirm all files are recognized: You should see output like: Timeline This change was made before Pyxle's public launch, so no existing user projects are affected. If you cloned an early version of the framework or followed prerelease tutorials, this guide covers the onetime migration needed.

When stdin is not a terminal (CI, pipes), it never prompts — it uses the flags and defaults below. Argument / Flag Default Description Project directory to create. Use (or omit) to scaffold into the current directory and derive the name from it. / Overwrite an existing directory (or scaffold into a nonempty current directory). / Project template. Only is supported today (other values error). / prompt → off Set up Tailwind CSS v4 (wired into Vite). / prompt → off Set up shadcn/ui (implies ). Import alias for project modules (e.g. ). / Accept all defaults without prompting (no Tailwind, no shadcn, default alias). / Run and after scaffolding. Examples: Install Python and Node.js dependencies. Argument / Flag Default Description Project directory / Install Python deps via / Install Node deps via Pass to , for externallymanaged (PEP 668) environments without a virtualenv. Use with care. Examples: Outside a virtualenv, warns about PEP 668 ("externallymanagedenvironment") and recommends creating a venv first; pass to install anyway. Start the development server with hot reload. Argument / Flag Default Description Project directory Starlette server bind address Starlette server port Vite dev server bind address Vite dev server port / Enable debug mode Number of persistent SSR worker processes ( = perrequest subprocess mode) Path to / Print merged configuration before starting / Autostart the legacy standalone Tailwind v3 CLI watcher when a handwritten is present. Tailwind v4 projects (the scaffold default when you opt into Tailwind) run through the plugin and ignore this flag. / Periodically print a live observability panel (request/SSR metrics) to the terminal / Restore full output: the raw Vite log firehose, debuglevel internals, and server logs in the browser console. Equivalent to the global . Examples: What it does: 1. Loads configuration from + environment variables + CLI flags 2. Compiles files into Python and JSX modules 3. Starts the Vite dev server for React hot reload (Tailwind v4 compiles here via the plugin) 4. Starts the legacy Tailwind v3 watcher only if a handwritten is detected 5. Starts the Starlette ASGI server 6. Watches for file changes and recompiles automatically Console output. By default prints a clean, curated console — a startup summary (the local URL, the Vite URL, the route count, and the total "ready in X ms"), a concise oneline notice per incremental rebuild (), and any warnings or errors. The raw linebyline Vite firehose and debuglevel internals are hidden. Pass (or , or the global ) to restore the full output when troubleshooting. Genuine signal — errors, warnings, the URLs, and rebuild success/failure — is always shown, at every verbosity. Server logs in the browser console (dev only). While is running, your serverside output (from loaders, actions, and your own modules) is forwarded to the browser devtools console, prefixed and mapped to the matching method ( → , → , → ). This lets you follow server logs without leaving the browser. By default only and above from your own loggers are forwarded; additionally forwards records and the framework's own internal loggers. This is strictly a development feature — it never runs under and never appears in the production bundle. Build productionready assets. Argument / Flag Default Description Project directory Path to Output directory for build artifacts / Reuse cached artifacts / Prerender loaderless, nondynamic pages to HTML at build time (SSG) — see Caching / Print a JS/CSS bundlesize report (raw + gzip, largest first) after the build — see Build Optimization Examples: Serve a production build (without Vite). Argument / Flag Default Description Project directory Bind address Port number Directory with production artifacts / Skip running build first Path to / Serve static assets directly from Pyxle Number of persistent SSR worker processes, per server worker ( = autosize to CPU cores, capped at 4) / Number of server worker processes (one per CPU core); enables multicore serving; autodetects from CPU cores Examples: With (N 1), Pyxle serves the build across uvicorn worker processes. Each is an independent server with its own SSR pool, so applies per worker (total render processes = ). See the deployment guide for sizing guidance. Validate files, configuration, and dependencies without starting the server. Each file is checked at three levels: Python syntax (via ) and Pyxle structural rules (loader/action shape, , …). Python semantics (via pyflakes): undefined names — e.g. a symbol you but never imported — unused imports, redefinitions. Compilerinjected runtime names (, , , , …) are recognized, so the idiomatic patterns never read as undefined. JSX syntax (via Babel): unclosed tags/expressions, mismatched braces, TypeScript in a client block, and duplicate (which Babel accepts but the build rejects). As of 0.7.0 the JSX level works out of the box — , which provides the Babelbased checker, ships with the framework. On earlier versions it required the extra (); without it, the JSX check reported itself unavailable. What a green check proves — and what it doesn't. is a static gate: it validates syntax, Python semantics, and JSX syntax. It does not render your pages, so a mistake that only exists at runtime — a component reading when the loader returns , a loader whose query fails against the real database — surfaces when the page renders, not here. Treat a clean check as "this compiles"; loading the page under (or a test that requests it) remains the runtime proof. Argument / Flag Default Description Project directory Path to Example output: Errors are reported per file as , with the file path on the next line (every file is checked, so one broken file never aborts the scan): Exit code is when clean, when any error is found. Run TypeScript typechecking on compiled JSX output. Requires in your . Runs against the compiled JSX in . Argument / Flag Default Description Project directory Path to Display the route table for your project. Argument / Flag Default Description Project directory Path to Output as JSON Example output: Use for machinereadable output. Generate an OpenAPI 3.1 document from your request models. For every action that declares a Pydantic body parameter, Pyxle emits a operation with the model's JSON Schema as the request body and a structured validation response; actions without a model get a permissive object body. Argument / Flag Default Description Project directory Path to / Write the schema to this file (default: stdout) OpenAPI OpenAPI The schema is derived from runtime introspection of the compiled action modules, so it always matches what the dispatcher actually validates. Requires the extra (); the command exits with an error if Pydantic isn't installed or a page module can't be imported.

searchIndex.title: Configuration Reference
searchIndex.description: Pyxle is configured via `pyxle.config.json` in the project root. All fields are optional -- sensible defaults are used when omitted.
searchIndex.searchText: Configuration Reference Pyxle is configured via in the project root. All fields are optional sensible defaults are used when omitted. Full schema Directory settings Key Type Default Description Directory containing page routes and API files Directory for static assets served at Directory for compiled build artifacts Server settings Key Type Default Description Starlette server bind address Starlette server port (165535) Key Type Default Description Vite dev server bind address Vite dev server port (165535) Key Type Default Description Enable debug mode (hot reload, detailed errors) Middleware Applicationlevel middleware classes applied to every request. Each entry is a string pointing to a Starlettecompatible middleware class. Routelevel hooks applied to specific route types. Key Type Description Hooks for page routes Hooks for API routes Hooks for endpoints. An action hook should reject with a JSON / (not an HTML redirect) — see the Middleware guide. Styling Key Type Description CSS files inlined on every page (relative to project root) JS files loaded on every page (relative to project root) CORS Key Type Default Description Allowed origins. CORS is disabled if empty. Allowed HTTP methods. An empty or omitted list falls back to the six defaults — it is not a way to disallow every method. Allowed request headers Allow cookies and auth headers Preflight cache duration (seconds) CSRF Key Type Default Description Enable CSRF protection auto: CSRF cookie name; unset → namespaced by the app's bind port CSRF header name Set flag on cookie attribute (, , ) Paths exempt from CSRF checks (matched on segment boundaries) Cookie naming. Browsers scope cookies to a host, ignoring the port, so a fixed cookie name would collide between two Pyxle apps on the same host (e.g. two dev servers on ) — each would overwrite the other's token and every action in the other app would fail with . When is unset, Pyxle namespaces the cookie with the app's bind port (), which is stable per app in development and behind a production reverse proxy. Set to pin an explicit name instead. Full details and the upgrade note: Security → CSRF protection. Exemption matching. An entry matches its exact path and anything beneath it at a segment boundary: exempts and , but not . Request paths are normalised first (/ resolved, repeated slashes collapsed), so is checked as . An entry of (or an empty string) is ignored rather than disabling CSRF sitewide — use to disable CSRF entirely. Shorthand to disable: Edge caching Mark page routes that render no peruser data as publicly cacheable. A matched page is served (plus a window) instead of the default , so a shared cache a CDN or reverse proxy can absorb the traffic instead of your origin. It's Pyxle's configdriven equivalent of Next.js perroute revalidation. Each key is a route pattern; each value is the sharedcache lifetime in seconds: Form Example Meaning Integer shorthand for the exact path Prefix wildcard for and anything beneath it Object form Same as the shorthand, with room to grow Matching. An exact path wins over a wildcard; among wildcards the longest (most specific) prefix wins. A pattern must be an absolute path. A wildcard () matches the bare prefix () and any segment beneath it (), but not a path that merely shares the string (). A cacheable response carries no CSRF cookie. A peruser must never ride on a response that a shared cache can replay to other users and most CDNs refuse to cache any response that sets a cookie at all. So on a cacheable route Pyxle omits the CSRF cookie, which means any reachable from that route must be exempt via [](#csrf). Only mark routes that render no peruser state and whose actions are safe to exempt. Key Type Default Description Map of route pattern → sharedcache lifetime. Empty = no shared caching (every page stays ). \ Lifetime in seconds (≥ 0) for the matched route. The objectform key is (camelCase); is accepted as a legacy alias. Turning it on at the edge. These headers make a response eligible for shared caching, but some CDNs don't cache HTML by default. On Cloudflare, you must also add a Cache Rule ("Cache Everything") for the cached paths the headers alone are necessary but not sufficient. See Deployment → CDN and edge caching. Navigation Pyxle's client keeps an inmemory navigation cache so clientside navigation and the data prefetched when a scrolls into view or is hovered resolves instantly instead of refetching the loader. The page you land on is seeded into this cache from the server render, so its loader never runs a second time on the initial load. Key Type Default Description Client navigation/prefetch cache settings. Lifetime in seconds (≥ 0) that a prefetched or seeded static, loaderless page stays fresh in the client navigation cache. disables navigation caching for those pages. A route listed in the [](#edgecaching) block reuses its edgecache TTL as its navigationcache lifetime, overriding for that route so a page's client freshness matches how long a CDN would serve it. (Consequence: raising a route's TTL also lengthens its edge cache, so purge the CDN on deploy which you should do regardless.) A dynamic page a loader with no entry is not navigationcached at all (it renders ), so back/forward always refetches and does not apply to it; add a entry to opt such a page into navigation caching. After a mutation, the client router's drops a cached entry so the next navigation refetches. Rate limiting A builtin tokenbucket limiter. Disabled by default (). Set to a positive number to cap how fast a single client may call the app. Key Type Default Description Bucket capacity = max burst. disables the limiter. Seconds for a full bucket to refill, so the sustained rate is per second. Fractional seconds are allowed (the value is stored as a float). The JSON key is . Paths skipped by the limiter, matched on segment boundaries (same rules as [](#csrf)). Use for health checks and metrics scrapes. Key clients by the first hop instead of the socket IP. Only enable behind a trusted proxy — a client can otherwise spoof the header to dodge the limit. Burst vs. sustained. is the burst a client can spend at once; the bucket then refills at per second, so the longrun average is bounded without rejecting brief spikes. A blocked request gets with a header. Multiworker caveat. The bucket store is inmemory and perprocess, so under each worker enforces the limit independently and the effective global cap is . For a single shared limit, ratelimit at your reverse proxy or load balancer. See the middleware guide. Observability Request correlation IDs and timing. Both are on by default generating an id and reading two timestamps per request is submicrosecond and adds no I/O, and every request gets a correlation key surfaced as the response header and on (readable from any loader or ). See the Observability guide. Key Type Default Description \ Request observability settings. disables requestid and timing; enables both. Assign each request a correlation id, expose it on , and echo it as the response header. The request/response header carrying the correlation id. Honour a wellformed incoming id from instead of generating one. Leave off unless behind a trusted reverse proxy echoing a clientsupplied id is a loginjection/spoofing vector. Even when on, an incoming id must match or it is replaced. Measure wallclock request duration (to the response start). Expose a Prometheus metrics endpoint (request/render/loader/action durations and pagecache hit ratio). Off by default it exposes internal state. Path the metrics endpoint is served at when enabled. \ Optional bearer token: when set, the endpoint requires (compared in constant time). Emit one structured accesslog line per request (, , , , ). Off by default so it doesn't surprise existing log scrapers. (humanreadable) or (one JSON object per line). Accesslogger level (////). Emit OpenTelemetry spans for requests, SSR renders, loaders, and actions. Requires the extra;

Tell the client router to evict its cached navigation payloads for before the caller's next navigation. Used by handlers that mutate data the client has already cached elsewhere. The helper accepts either a plain dict (the usual return shape) or a Starlette / . In both cases Pyxle attaches an header to the outgoing response. and read this header automatically and call the clientside [](clientapi.md#invalidateurl) for each listed URL — so mutationdriven cache invalidation works endtoend with no extra wiring on the caller. Parameters: Parameter Type Description The action's return value. Modified in place and returned. URLs to invalidate. Empty / duplicate values are filtered. Returns the argument (possibly with a header or sentinel key added) so you can in one line. Background tasks — Fireandforget background work that runs inprocess on the app's event loop. See the Background Tasks guide. Schedule on the app's bounded worker pool and return immediately. Coroutine functions are awaited; plain callables run in a thread. Usable from any loader or action while the app is running. Raises When Called outside a running Pyxle app (no active queue) The bounded queue is at capacity (sustained overload) A task that raises is logged ( logger) and never takes a worker down. The queue is perprocess — under each worker has its own, and queued work is lost on restart. For durable or crossworker jobs, use a real job queue (Celery / ARQ / Dramatiq) — see the guide. Postresponse tasks in an Inside an , is a Starlette [](https://www.starlette.io/background/); runs after the response is sent. Returning from the action is shorthand for a single such task. WebSockets — A page that exports a modulescope also serves a WebSocket at its path. provides the pub/sub and auth helpers those handlers use. See the WebSockets guide for the full walkthrough. The broker defaults to the appscoped on (one per process — see the guide's multiworker caveat). Implement the protocol ( / / ) for a Redis/NATS backend. The auth middleware never runs for WebSocket scope, so a handler that needs the user must call this (it reads the session cookie off the handshake). searchIndex.description: `pyxle-db` is Pyxle's official database plugin over **SQLite, PostgreSQL, and MySQL**. It offers two first-class paths: an **explicit-SQL** API (portable placeholders, a uniform `Row` type, checksum-t
searchIndex.searchText: pyxledb is Pyxle's official database plugin over SQLite, PostgreSQL, and MySQL. It offers two firstclass paths: an explicitSQL API (portable placeholders, a uniform type, checksumtracked migrations — you write SQL, it makes that SQL portable) and an optional SQLAlchemy ORM path. Either way, every loader and action gets a requestscoped database handle, and writes commit or roll back automatically. Version 0.3.0. SQLite needs nothing beyond the package; PostgreSQL, MySQL, and the SQLAlchemy ORM ship as extras (the base install stays SQLAlchemyfree). Every backendspecific behaviour documented here is enforced by test suites that run against real PostgreSQL 16 and MySQL 8 servers in CI. Install The extra includes because MySQL 8's default authentication requires it at connect time. Quickstart Add the plugin to (see the plugins guide for how plugin loading works): At startup the plugin opens the database, applies any pending migrations, and registers the shared instance. Loaders and actions reach it through : Plugin settings All settings are optional — with none, you get SQLite at . There is no init step: on first startup the plugin creates the directory and the database file itself, then applies any pending migrations. (Plugins built on pyxledb inherit this — pyxleauth's user records land in this same file until you point at a real server.) Key Default What it does SQLite file path, relative to the project root. — Full database URL (takes precedence over ). Supports indirection. Directory of migration files, applied at startup. Milliseconds to wait for a SQLite file to appear before failing (useful when another process creates it). Wrap each unsafemethod (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) request's writes in one autocommit transaction; set to manage every transaction by hand. — Enable the SQLAlchemy ORM path, e.g. . Requires the extra. See The ORM path below. All of the above are keys inside the plugin's object (not at the config root). Never commit credentials. The setting supports indirection — the committed config names an environment variable, the deploy environment supplies the secret: Startup fails with a clear error if the named variable is unset, rather than silently falling back to SQLite. The plugin registers three services: (the ), (the connection URL with credentials redacted), and — for SQLite — (the resolved file path, registered as a ). Note this is distinct from the property, which returns a redactionsafe . Database URLs A bare filesystem path is also accepted and treated as SQLite. Server backends accept pool sizing as URL options: Other PostgreSQL options (such as ) pass through to asyncpg as server settings. The MySQL backend pins every pooled session to UTC (), so columns and are never shifted through the server's system time zone. Queries and rows Four query methods, identical on every backend: returns the affected row count. Every read returns the same type — immutable, accessible by index and by column name: is the bridge to whatever model layer you prefer; no integration code needed: Placeholders Always write . pyxledb rewrites it to each backend's native style — for SQLite, / for PostgreSQL, for MySQL — so the SQL in your codebase never forks per engine. When you need a literal question mark (PostgreSQL's JSON operators), escape it as : The rewriter is literalaware: inside string literals, quoted identifiers, comments, and dollarquoted bodies is never touched, so data can't become SQL structure during translation. Transactions The transaction object carries the full query surface (, , , , ). Two escape hatches are SQLiteonly and raise on server backends: and — prefer , which works everywhere. Requestscoped access and autotransactions With the plugin installed, every loader and action gets a lazy database handle on — no import, no service lookup. A request that never queries opens no connection. Loader and action return values are serialized with , so they must be plain JSON types. A is not JSONserializable — convert it with (or ) before returning, as above. Returning raw objects raises a at render time. On an unsafe method (///) the request's writes run inside one transaction that commits when the action succeeds and rolls back when it fails — where "fails" means the action raised (or any exception), which Pyxle turns into a non2xx response. You never call /, and a failed action never leaves a partial write behind. / run readonly. Opt out per action with (then manage yourself), or appwide with . The ORM path (SQLAlchemy) Prefer an ORM? Install and set in the plugin settings. Models subclass ; loaders and actions get a requestscoped on under the same autotransaction rules: SQLAlchemy errors surface as the same pyxledb error types as the explicitSQL path. The base install stays SQLAlchemyfree. The CLI The CLI reads the same + the app uses. Pick one migration tool per app: the checksum migrator for explicitSQL, Alembic for the ORM. Datetimes One contract on all three engines, in both directions: Reads always return timezoneaware UTC objects — including from naive columns, which are interpreted as UTC. Binds accept either naive datetimes (assumed UTC) or aware ones (converted to UTC before binding). Without this, the engines disagree sharply: asyncpg rejects aware datetimes for columns outright, and MySQL's driver would silently serialise an aware datetime's foreign wall clock. pyxledb normalises at the backend boundary so application code never thinks about it. Migrations Migrations are plain SQL files in the configured directory, named : Rules the migrator enforces: Applied exactly once, atomically. Each migration's statements and its trackingtable insert commit together or not at all. Checksumtracked. Editing an alreadyapplied migration is detected and rejected — write a new migration instead. Perdialect overrides. replaces the base file on that backend (the example above uses MySQLspecific DDL while SQLite and PostgreSQL share the base file). An override with no base file is a backendonly migration. Namespaced tracking. The default tracking table is ; libraries that bring their own migrations onto your database (pyxleauth does) use a private tracking table via so two migration histories never see each other as drift. The plugin applies pending migrations at startup. You can also drive the directly: Writing portable schemas These rules are proven against real PostgreSQL and MySQL servers — the live conformance suites enforce them: for every key or indexed column. MySQL cannot index bare (error 1170). SQLite and PostgreSQL treat exactly like , so nothing is lost. Keep for payloads. columns are fine on SQLite and PostgreSQL. On MySQL prefer via a perdialect override: MySQL's is capped at 2038 and rounds to whole seconds. MySQL has no . Create indexes in migrations (they run exactly once) or probe first. Spell out inserted values instead of relying on column s, which drift subtly between engines. Errors One hierarchy regardless of driver — application code never imports , , or : Exception Raised when Base class for everything below. Constraint violation (unique, foreign key, …). Connection refused, pool exhaustion, server gone. Bad URL, bad pool option, unset variable. SQLiteonly call on a server backend. found no row. / Malformed or edited migrations. The DatabaseLike contract Plugins that need a database (like pyxleauth) don't depend on the class — they bind to the protocol: , , , an asynccontextmanager , and a property. Any object satisfying it, registered as the service, can stand in — an adapter over another engine, a test fake. is the reference implementation, and the protocol is : The full replacement contract (error translation, dialect names, datetime semantics) is documented in the protocol's docstring and exercised by pyxleauth's contract test suite. Using it without the plugin Scripts, tests, and workers can open a database directly: , , , and expose the backend name, parsed configuration, SQLite file path, and a perinstance query
