searchIndex.title: Server-side rendering
searchIndex.description: Server-side rendering (SSR) is the process of turning a `PageRoute` and an HTTP request into an HTML response. It's the part of the framework that runs on every page load — so it has to be fast, robus
searchIndex.searchText: Serverside rendering Serverside rendering (SSR) is the process of turning a and an HTTP request into an HTML response. It's the part of the framework that runs on every page load — so it has to be fast, robust, and predictable. It's also the part that touches both Python (the loader) and Node.js (the React renderer), so a fair bit of the complexity lives in the bridge between the two languages. This doc walks through the SSR pipeline stage by stage. By the end you'll know exactly what happens between "Starlette receives a GET" and "the browser hydrates", including the worker pool protocol, the head merge algorithm, and the streaming response strategy. Files (): File Lines What it does 720 Orchestrates loader, render, head, document assembly 310 Wraps Node.js component rendering (subprocess or worker pool) 330 Persistent SSR workers, NDJSON over stdin/stdout 420 Merges head elements from 4 sources, deduplicates, sanitizes 455 HTML document assembly (dev + production modes) 50 Reexports the public surface The interesting code lives in , , and . The rest is supporting infrastructure. The pipeline at a glance Source: . If anything goes wrong at any stage, error handling kicks in: A triggers the nearest boundary. A (the React component crashes during SSR) also tries the error boundary. An unexpected exception falls through to , which renders the developer overlay (in dev) or a generic page (in production). Stage 1: importing the compiled server module The page handler closure inside knows the for the current URL. The first thing it does is import the compiled Python module at : In dev mode, before importing, Pyxle purges any cached version of this module from : This is the hotreload mechanism. When you save a file: 1. The watcher rebuilds the artifacts. 2. The file on disk has new content. 3. The next request purges the old cached module. 4. Python's import machinery reads the new file from disk. 5. The new code runs. In production mode (), modules are imported once at startup and never reimported. This avoids the perrequest import overhead and is consistent with the immutable nature of a deployed build. Source: . Stage 2: running the loader Once the module is imported, Pyxle finds the function tagged with (set by the decorator) and calls it: Loaders are always async — the parser refuses to compile a sync . They take exactly one positional argument named (a Starlette object). They return a JSON serializable dict. A few invariants hold: The loader name comes from the metadata. Pyxle doesn't for "the function with " at runtime — it looked up the name once at compile time and stored it in . This means runtime startup doesn't have to walk the module looking for decorated functions. The loader is awaited directly. Pyxle does not wrap it in , doesn't add timeouts, doesn't catch exceptions outside of the documented error types. The loader is your code, and it runs as your code. The result must be JSONserializable. If you return a , a , or a custom class, the next stage () will raise . Pyxle catches that and routes it through the same error pipeline as any other render failure. If the loader raises , Pyxle catches it and looks for the nearest boundary up the directory tree. If found, it renders the error page with the error context as a prop. If not, it renders the default error document. If the loader raises any other exception, Pyxle treats it the same way but logs it as an unexpected failure and includes the stack trace in dev mode. Source: . Stage 3: resolving the head The of the HTML response is assembled from up to four sources, in order of increasing priority: 1. Layout JSX blocks — collected at registrybuild time from (and ) ancestors of the current page. 2. The page's variable — Python string, list of strings, or callable returning either. If it's a callable, Pyxle invokes it with the loader's data. 3. The page's JSX blocks — extracted from the page's JSX section by the parser at compile time. 4. Runtime registrations — rare; used by some advanced helpers that register head elements during SSR. Each source contributes a list of HTML strings (e.g. ). The function () merges them with deduplication and sanitization. Deduplication The merge identifies "the same" element by its deduplication key, which depends on the tag: Tag Dedupe key tag name (so only one survives) "charset" (singleton) "canonical" (singleton) (manual key) Anything else no key — always included If two elements share a key, the higherpriority one wins. So a in the page's JSX overrides a in the layout's JSX. A in the page's variable overrides one in the layout. Within the same priority tier, deeper nesting wins (page over parent layout, child layout over root layout). This matches the behaviour of React Helmet and other head libraries — your innermost layer is the most specific, so it should win. Source: . Sanitization Every element passes through (), which parses the element and rebuilds it through a strict allowlist: 1. Enforces a tag allowlist. Only , , , , and may appear in the head; anything else (, , , ...) is dropped entirely. 2. HTMLescapes every attribute value. Elements are rebuilt from their parsed attributes with values escaped, so a quote inside interpolated data can't break out of its attribute and inject markup. Trailing markup injected after a breakout is discarded. 3. Escapes and inside . The HTML spec says title content shouldn't contain raw HTML, but if a user ships dynamic title content from a loader, an attacker could try to inject a closing tag and break out of the title element. Pyxle escapes both characters defensively and discards anything after the closing . 4. Strips event handler attributes. Any attribute starting with (, , , etc.) is removed. Head elements don't run JavaScript, so these are pure XSS vectors. 5. Neutralizes , , and URLs in , , and attributes. A would actually execute that script in some browsers; Pyxle replaces the URL with an empty string. 6. Rejects — a datacontrolled refresh target is an openredirect vector. Void tags (, ) are reserialized selfclosing (), so output markup may differ cosmetically from the input. The sanitization is always on — there's no optout. Inline / content is the one deliberate passthrough: it is treated as trusted author code and preserved verbatim, so it must never contain interpolated user data. Stage 4: rendering the React component This is the most complex part of the SSR pipeline. We need to take a React component (which is JavaScript) and produce an HTML string from it (also JavaScript), all from inside Python. The mechanism is (), which delegates to either: Perrequest subprocess mode () — spawn a fresh Node.js subprocess for every render Worker pool mode (default, ) — keep N persistent Node.js workers alive Each mode has its own Node.js entry script: subprocess mode runs (one render per process), while the worker pool runs the longlived (a persistent render loop). Both share the same esbuild + React rendering core. The render protocol A render request is a JSON message: The Node.js worker: 1. Bundles the component with esbuild. Imports get inlined into a single JavaScript string. esbuild is fast — typically under 30ms even for nontrivial component trees. The bundle is cached per so repeat requests for the same page reuse the bundle. 2. Evaluates the bundle in a fresh context. The default export is the React component function. 3. Calls . This produces a plain HTML string from the component tree. (A request flagged instead drives and replies with a sequence of // frames sharing the request id — see Streaming SSR. The buffered path described here remains the default for every nonstreaming, cacheable, and statically generated render.) 4. Collects head elements emitted by blocks. When the component renders a block, the runtime captures the children and returns them separately (so they can be hoisted into the document head). 5. Replies with another JSON message. The reply: The field lets the worker pool match response

searchIndex.title: Build and serve
searchIndex.description: `pyxle dev` is what you run during development. `pyxle build` and `pyxle serve` are what you run for production. They share most of their code with the dev server but make a few important changes:
searchIndex.searchText: Build and serve is what you run during development. and are what you run for production. They share most of their code with the dev server but make a few important changes: Aspect + Vite dev server on :5173 onetime bundle, then no Vite Source compilation incremental on file change full rebuild, all at once File watcher running not running HMR / React Refresh enabled disabled Module reloading perrequest purge imported once at startup Error responses full stack trace + dev overlay generic Asset serving proxy to Vite static files from Route discovery metadata files in Port 8000 by default 8000 by default This doc explains the production pipeline: what does, what artifacts it produces, and how runs them. Files (): File Lines What it does 343 The orchestrator 193 Invokes and parses its output 26 Loads 7 Reexports The CLI commands themselves live in : is around line 400, is around line 517. What actually does When you run , the pipeline executes six steps in order: Source: . The directory is the only thing your deployment needs. Once it exists, you can it on a server, copy it to a container image, push it to a CDN, or do anything else you'd do with a staticplusserver build. Step 1: Compile sources Steps 1 of is identical to the initial build of — same function, same calls, same layout. The compiler doesn't know or care that we're in production mode; it just produces artifacts. The result is the same three files per page in : Plus any layoutcomposed route modules in and any files copied to . If anything fails to compile (a parser error, an unresolved import, a missing decorator, etc.), the build aborts here and prints the error. Production builds are strict — there is no tolerant mode for . The first error stops everything. Step 2: Run Vite build () invokes Vite to bundle the clientside JavaScript. It tries multiple invocation strategies in order: 1. if has a script. 2. Local if installed. 3. as a fallback. 4. if no local install. Each candidate is validated with before use. If no Vite is available at all, the pipeline runs to restore dependencies, then retries. The Vite invocation passes: — Pyxle's autogenerated Vite config — produce a manifest JSON that maps source files to hashed bundle entries env var — sets the asset base path so Vite emits URLs like instead of Vite's output: — the manifest — bundled, codesplit, hashed JS — bundled, hashed CSS — Vite's default HTML output (we ignore this; Pyxle generates its own HTML at request time) Vite logs are streamed to the console with a prefix. If the exit code is nonzero, Pyxle raises with the captured stderr. Step 3: Load Vite's manifest Vite's manifest looks like this: Each entry tells you the bundled file path, the list of imported chunks (so you can preload them), and the direct CSS dependencies. The interesting part is the import chain. imports , which itself has CSS. To get the complete CSS list for , we need to walk the imports recursively and collect all arrays. Otherwise we'd ship pages with missing styles from shared modules. () does this walk. It uses a visited set to handle cycles (Vite shouldn't produce cycles, but defense in depth) and returns the deduplicated list of CSS files for each entry. Step 4: Build the page manifest Vite's manifest is keyed by source file. Pyxle's is keyed by route: () iterates the metadata registry, looks up each page's bundled assets in the Vite manifest, walks the import chain to collect CSS, and emits one entry per route. Aliases (from optional catchalls) get their own entry pointing at the same data: The page manifest is written to and is the source of truth for production routing. The dev server's metadata registry is built from ; the production server's metadata registry is built from this single file. Step 5: Copy into The final layout under is: The and directories are direct copies of and . The directory is Vite's output () copied verbatim. is a copy of your project's directory (if it exists). These are static assets that ship to the browser unchanged: favicons, images, robots.txt, etc. The double nesting is intentional — Vite's output naturally lives under a subdirectory of its base path, and Pyxle preserves that. The serving layer is configured to mount it at the right URL prefix (). What does Once you have a directory, runs it. The serve command: 1. Loads with . This is the single most important setting that flips the framework into production mode. 2. Loads as the route source. 3. Builds a from the manifest entries. 4. Creates a from the registry. 5. Spawns the SSR worker pool. Same code as dev mode. 6. Builds a Starlette app with the same factory. The factory checks and includes the (productiononly) and skips the Vite proxy middleware (which would have nothing to talk to). 7. Runs uvicorn to serve the Starlette app. Source: . The result is a process that: Listens on port 8000 (or whatever you pass). Imports each compiled module once at startup, not perrequest. Serves , , and routes via mounts. Handles page routes the same way does — same SSR pipeline, same loader execution, same component rendering, same head merging. Responds to errors with opaque generic pages instead of the developer overlay. Why is the SSR worker pool the same in production? Because the rendering work doesn't change between dev and prod. The React component is the same JavaScript. esbuild bundles it the same way. produces the same output. The cost of running React on the server is identical. The only thing that changes is how often you pay for it: in dev, the worker is mostly idle and serves your singledeveloper requests. In production, the worker pool needs to scale to handle real traffic — which is why the recommended worker count for production is the number of CPU cores, not 1. You can adjust: …to spin up four persistent Node.js workers. Roundrobin dispatch gives you fourway SSR parallelism within one Pyxle process. Stateless processes, scale horizontally Pyxle's serve process is stateless: nothing is stored inprocess between requests. This means you can run multiple Pyxle instances behind a load balancer and they'll all serve the same content. The scaling story is "run more processes" — not "run more threads" or "tune a connection pool." Configuration overrides for production builds the production config with three overrides: Source: . is the critical one. It turns off: The hotreload purge The dev error overlay (replaced with opaque generic responses) The Vite client tag in the document The React Refresh preamble The WebSocket overlay endpoint It also turns on: GZip middleware Production asset path resolution via Streaming responses use the production document shell The full list of what controls is scattered across , , and — search the codebase for to see every gate. Other CLI flags for — bind port (default 8000) — bind host (default 127.0.0.1; use 0.0.0.0 for "listen on all interfaces", which you'll want behind a reverse proxy) — where to read the build output from (default: in the current directory) — skip the implicit and use the existing asis. Useful when the build artifacts come from CI and you just want to run them. — force a fresh build before serving (the default) — whether to serve and directly. Disable this if you're putting Pyxle behind a CDN that handles static assets. — number of persistent Node.js workers (default 1) What's in a deployable artifact? If you want to deploy a Pyxle app, the artifact is the directory plus the Python source for any files outside (your shared utilities, your dependencies, your ). A typical deployment looks like: The deployment process: 1. On the build machine: 2. Copy , , and the Python source to the server (or build a container image). 3. On the server: . Node.js isn't required for serving unless you have SSR workers spawning... wait, scratch that. Node.js is required — the SSR pipeline runs React on Node.js workers. You need Node.js on the production server too. 4. Run . 5. Put it behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy, Cloudflare, ALB,

searchIndex.title: The runtime
searchIndex.description: The "runtime" of Pyxle is the **smallest module in the framework**. It contains two decorators (`@server` and `@action`), two exception classes (`LoaderError` and `ActionError`), and one small helper
searchIndex.searchText: The runtime The "runtime" of Pyxle is the smallest module in the framework. It contains two decorators ( and ), two exception classes ( and ), and one small helper () — and nothing else. The whole file is about 150 lines including blank lines and docstrings. This is on purpose. The runtime is what your application code imports — it's the contract between your code and the framework. A small contract means a small surface area for bugs, a small mental model for users, and a small attack surface for prompt injection through documentation. This doc is the shortest in the architecture section because there isn't much code to explain. But the design decisions behind the runtime are some of the most important in Pyxle, and they're worth understanding. File: (150 lines) What's in The core contract is small enough to reproduce here: That's the core. The one remaining export is — a small helper that tags a response with an header so the client router drops its cached navigation payloads for the given URLs after a mutation (see Server Actions). Two decorators, two exceptions, one helper, one . Why so small? Most web frameworks have a runtime that's enormous: a request context, a global state container, dependency injection, an event loop, middleware execution, response builders, error handlers, the works. Pyxle has none of those things in its runtime. The reason is the compiler does all the framework work, not the runtime. By the time your code runs at request time: The parser has already extracted your loader and action metadata. The compiler has already injected the runtime imports into the artifact. The dev server has already imported the compiled module. Starlette has already dispatched the request. The SSR pipeline has already validated everything. When the framework finally calls your decorated function, the function is just a function. There is no context manager wrapping it, no try/except catching its exceptions, no proxy object intercepting attribute access. It runs as if you'd called it yourself. This is the nomagic design principle from the architecture README, applied as literally as possible. What actually does Here's the entire implementation of : That's the whole decorator. It sets one attribute on the function and returns it. The function is the same function — same identity, same signature, same , same , same , same closure cells, same everything. You can call it directly without going through Pyxle: The framework's only use of is at parse time, in the compiler — and even then, the compiler reads it from the AST (by checking decorator names), not from the runtime. The attribute is set as a documentation aid for tooling and debuggers, not as a mechanism the framework relies on. works identically, with instead. Why decorators at all then? If doesn't do anything, why is it a decorator? Why not just a naming convention like Flask's ? Because the parser uses the decorator name to find loaders and actions in the AST. When the parser walks your file, it asks each function definition: "is one of your decorators called ?" If yes, it treats the function as a loader. Same for . Naming conventions () would work, but they have downsides: Multiple functions per file would conflict. If the convention is "the function named is the loader", you can't have helper functions named without confusion. With a decorator, you can name the function whatever you want. You'd lose the explicit "this is a Pyxle thing" signal. When you read someone else's code, immediately tells you "this is the framework's entry point". A naming convention is invisible. Refactoring tools can't track names as reliably as decorators. Renaming a function is easy; changing a magic name convention isn't. The decorator wins on every axis except one: it requires an explicit , and even that is autoinjected by the compiler so you don't actually have to write the import yourself. Pyxle in plain Python: You can write the import yourself. The compiler checks for an existing and skips the autoinjection if you've already added it. The autoinjection exists because most users won't bother, not because the framework rejects explicit imports. The exception types and are the only structured errors the runtime defines. Both follow the same shape: You raise them from inside your loader or action when something is wrong: When is raised: The framework catches it. It looks for the nearest boundary. It renders the boundary with the error context as a prop: . It returns the response with HTTP status . works the same way for functions, except it returns a JSON error response () instead of rendering an HTML boundary. The default differs: defaults to 500 because most loader failures are serverside bugs. defaults to 400 because most action failures are clientside input problems. You override the defaults with the keyword argument: Why two exception types? Because they're caught at different points in the request pipeline, they translate to different response shapes (HTML page vs JSON), and they trigger different debugging affordances (error boundary vs clientside error handler). A single would force the framework to disambiguate at catch time, and would force users to remember which fields apply to which context. Two types make the contract explicit. The zerodependency rule has zero imports from the rest of the framework. The only things it imports are: (a Python 3.x compatibility thing) (standard library) No , no , no , nothing. This is enforced by rule 5 (the module boundary rule). The reason is runtime.py is the only module that ends up imported by your application code. When you write: …you're pulling into your project's import graph. If runtime.py imported , your project would transitively depend on the parser, the AST module, the JSX import rewriter, the Babel subprocess wrapper, and so on. That's a huge dependency footprint for a module that ends up being five lines of metadatasetting code. The zerodependency rule keeps the runtime fast to import and easy to test. You can import in 5 milliseconds. You can test it without setting up a full Pyxle project. You can use it from nonPyxle code if you wanted to, just to mark functions for some other framework's use. How the framework actually finds your loader Given that the runtime decorators don't do anything at runtime, how does the framework find your loader when a request comes in? The answer: at parse time, not at runtime. When the parser processes , walks the Python AST looking for nodes whose contains a named or an ending in . The first match becomes the loader. Its name is recorded in the dataclass: This metadata flows into the (compiled), then into the artifact, then into the at startup, then into the dataclass. At request time, the page handler does: It looks up the loader by name. The name was recorded by the parser at compile time. The decorator's role is naming the metadata key, not modifying behaviour. This is why the runtime can be three lines per decorator: by the time the function runs, the framework already knows everything it needs to know about it. The decorator doesn't have to do work because the parser already did it. What if I need real request middleware? If you need to wrap loaders with crosscutting behaviour (auth, logging, rate limiting, etc.), Pyxle has two options: 1. Custom middleware — Starlette ASGI middleware declared in : This is the right place for things that should run on every request regardless of which loader is invoked. 2. Route hooks — Perroute policies declared in : This is the right place for things that should run for most page or API routes but not all of them. Both mechanisms wrap the request outside the loader, so the loader itself stays untouched. The runtime decorators don't have to participate. Source: , . What if I want to test my loader? Because doesn't wrap the function, the loader is a plain async function — so testing it is a direct call. A file isn't an importable Python module, though (it carries a JSX compone

searchIndex.title: The CLI
searchIndex.description: `pyxle` is the command-line entry point users actually type. It's a
searchIndex.searchText: The CLI is the commandline entry point users actually type. It's a Typer application with a small set of singlepurpose commands: Command Purpose Scaffold a new Pyxle project Install Python + Node dependencies Run the development server Compile and bundle for production Serve a production build Validate the project without serving Print the filebased route table TypeScriptcheck compiled JSX This doc explains what each command does, how flags and configuration flow from user input to the rest of the framework, and the design choices behind the CLI surface. Files: (1220 lines) — the Typer commands (115 lines) — scaffolding (100 lines) — copies template files (50 lines) — template registry (190 lines) — with structured output (500 lines) — schema and validation Configuration precedence Every Pyxle command reads configuration in the same order, from lowest to highest priority: 1. Builtin defaults — Hardcoded in field defaults (). 2. — JSON file at the project root. 3. Environment variables — Variables starting with override the corresponding config field. 4. CLI flags — Anything you pass on the command line wins. Higher priority overrides lower priority. So: …ends up running on port 8001 (CLI beats env beats file beats default). The mechanism is (), which returns a new frozen instance with selective field updates. Each layer applies overrides on top of the previous one. The CLI layer runs last: ignores values, so passing overrides while not passing leaves the lower layer alone. This is the standard "explicit wins, implicit yields" pattern. Source: . Scaffolds a new Pyxle project from a template. Under the hood: 1. Validates the name. Must be a nonempty string that's safe to use as a directory name. The directory must not exist (unless is passed). 2. Resolves the template. Defaults to . Templates live in inside the package — they're shipped with . 3. Renders the template files into the new directory. A composed from the user's choices maps each output path to a resource under , rendered with a context (project name, framework requirement, featureconditional dependency lines, import alias). The alwayspresent set includes: — a working "Hello, Pyxle" page — a sample API route — Vite 7 + React 19 (plus Tailwind/shadcn deps when chosen) — minimal config () — pinned version + — import alias + Viteecosystem config — favicon and a few static files — sensible defaults Featureconditional files: a Tailwind v4 CSS entry (or a plainCSS + CSSModules baseline when Tailwind is declined), and shadcn/ui's + when selected. There are no / files — Tailwind v4 runs through Vite. 4. Optionally installs dependencies. With (the default), runs and in the new directory. With , skips both — useful for CI or when you want to inspect the scaffold before installing. Source: , , . Why does the scaffold ship with the framework? A few alternatives we considered: Download from a Git repository. Requires the user to have network access and installed. Doesn't pin a version. Download from a CDN. Requires the user to have network access. Versions can drift. Generate from a template engine. Adds a runtime dependency on Jinja2 or similar. Templates become harder to read and modify. Shipping the scaffold as part of the Pyxle package means: It works offline. It's pinned to the framework version automatically. You can read it directly: to find the install path, then look at . You can fork it locally without affecting the upstream package. The downside is that updating the scaffold requires a Pyxle release, not a separate publish step. We think the tradeoff is worth it for betastage software where the install story matters more than ergonomics. Runs the development server. The full lifecycle is documented in The dev server, but the CLIspecific bits are: Flags Flag Default Effect Starlette bind host Starlette bind port Vite bind host Vite bind port Dev mode toggle Config file path from config Override SSR worker count auto Force/disable the legacy Tailwind v3 watcher What it does 1. Resolves the project root (CLI argument or current directory). 2. Loads , applies env vars, applies CLI flags. 3. Resolves global stylesheets and scripts via the styling helpers. 4. Builds a from the merged config. 5. Creates a instance and calls . The actual heavy lifting happens inside — spawning Vite, starting the SSR worker pool, building the Starlette app, starting the file watcher, running uvicorn. See The dev server. Source: . Tailwind handling Tailwind is optin at . When chosen, the scaffold uses Tailwind v4 via the plugin: the generated Vite config () adds the plugin whenever the project's depends on , so Tailwind compiles inside Vite's normal CSS pipeline with HMR — no config files, no separate process. A legacy path remains for handconfigured Tailwind v3 projects: if the CLI finds a in the project root (and no PostCSS config), it spawns a standalone process alongside Vite. The flag forces or disables that legacy watcher; it has no effect on Tailwind v4 (Viteplugin) projects. Source: , . Compiles and bundles the project for production. Detailed walkthrough in Build and serve. Flags Flag Default Effect Config file path (full rebuild) Skip unchanged files Output directory What it does 1. Loads config, applies env, applies CLI flags. 2. Builds a (yes, the same dataclass uses — reuses 100% of the dev server's settings structure). 3. Lazily imports (lazy because importing the build pipeline pulls in , , etc., and we want and to start fast). 4. Calls . 5. Prints a summary: pages, API routes, client assets. Source: . The lazy import pattern You'll notice uses lazy imports for the heavy modules: This is intentional. should not have to load the build pipeline. should not have to load Starlette. By deferring imports until they're actually needed, the CLI startup stays fast — typically under 100ms for or , even on cold imports. This pattern is documented in rule 16 (Lazy imports for heavy modules). Serves a production build. See Build and serve for the full walkthrough. Flags Flag Default Effect Starlette bind host Starlette bind port Config file path Where to read the build from Skip the implicit Serve and from config Override SSR worker count What it does 1. Loads config, applies env, applies CLI flags. 2. Forces in the resolved config. This is the single most important production override. 3. Optionally runs first (the default — set to use existing artifacts). 4. Loads to populate the route registry. 5. Builds the Starlette app via (same factory as ). 6. Spawns uvicorn to serve the app. Source: . is for CI In a typical CI pipeline: Then, on the production server: tells Pyxle "the artifacts are already in , don't rebuild them." This separates "compile time" from "run time" cleanly. In dev, you usually omit the flag and let build for you. Validates the project without starting a server. This is the linter / precommit hook command. What it checks 1. The project structure exists. must be a directory. 2. The config is valid. (if present) must parse and pass schema validation. 3. Node.js dependencies are present. should exist (warning if missing). 4. Every file parses cleanly. Both Python and JSX halves. Tolerant mode The fourth check is the interesting one. runs the parser in tolerant mode: Source: . Tolerant mode means the parser collects every diagnostic in every file in a single pass instead of stopping at the first error. This is the right behaviour for a linter — you want to see all your errors at once, not one error at a time. adds Babelbacked JSX validation on top, so JSX syntax errors (unclosed tags, mismatched braces, invalid expressions) are also caught. This is the only Pyxle command that runs Babel validation by default; and skip it because Vite catches JSX errors at bundle time. Defensive perfile wrapping Each perfile parse is wrapped in a : This is defenseindepth. Tolerant mode shouldn't raise — it collects errors instead — but a future parser bug could throw an unexpected exception. The defensive wrap catches

searchIndex.title: SSR Pipeline
searchIndex.description: This document explains how Pyxle renders pages on the server. Understanding the SSR pipeline is useful for debugging render issues and optimising performance.
searchIndex.searchText: SSR Pipeline This document explains how Pyxle renders pages on the server. Understanding the SSR pipeline is useful for debugging render issues and optimising performance. Overview When a request hits a page route, Pyxle: 1. Resolves the route matches the URL to a file 2. Imports the server module the compiled Python code from the section 3. Runs the loader executes the function with the request 4. Resolves HEAD elements evaluates the variable (static or dynamic) 5. Renders the component runs React serverside rendering via Node.js 6. Merges head elements deduplicates and sanitises head elements from all sources 7. Assembles the document builds the HTML document with hydration scripts 8. Streams the response sends the HTML to the client Component rendering The React component is rendered serverside using Node.js. Pyxle supports two rendering modes: Subprocess mode (default when workers = 0) Each render spawns a new Node.js process: 1. esbuild bundles the component and its imports into a single file 2. Node.js executes the bundle with from 3. The rendered HTML and any extracted head elements are returned as JSON 4. The subprocess exits This mode is simple but has startup overhead (200400ms per request). Worker pool mode (default: 1 worker) Persistent Node.js processes eliminate startup cost: 1. On server start, Pyxle launches N worker processes 2. Workers stay running, communicating via stdin/stdout with newlinedelimited JSON 3. Each render sends a request to an available worker 4. The worker bundles the component with esbuild, renders it, and returns the result 5. Latency drops to 3080ms (esbuild bundling only) Configure workers: Head element pipeline Head elements come from three sources, listed in order of increasing priority: 1. Layout blocks from files up the directory tree 2. Page variable from the Python section of the page's file 3. Page blocks from components in the page's JSX The merge process: 1. All sources are split into individual elements using 2. Each element is sanitised (event handlers stripped, title content escaped, dangerous URLs removed) 3. Elements are deduplicated by tagspecific keys (title by tag name, meta by name/property, etc.) 4. Higherpriority sources override lowerpriority ones for duplicate keys 5. Nondeduplicatable elements (inline scripts, custom tags) are all included Document assembly The final HTML document is structured as: In dev mode, the document includes the Vite client script and React Refresh preamble for hot module replacement. Streaming For production builds with a manifest, Pyxle uses to send the document in chunks: 1. The HTML prefix (up to ) is sent immediately 2. The serverrendered body HTML follows 3. The suffix (hydration scripts and closing tags) completes the stream This allows the browser to start parsing and rendering before the full document is ready. Error handling Errors at each stage are handled differently: Stage Error type Behaviour Loader Renders nearest with error context Loader Other exceptions Renders or default error document HEAD evaluation Renders or default error document Component render Renders or default error document In dev mode, the error overlay shows the error with breadcrumbs indicating which stage failed. Clientside navigation When navigating between pages clientside, Pyxle requests a JSON payload for the same URL instead of full HTML — signalled by a request header, not a query param: This returns: The client runtime swaps the component props and updates the document head without a full page reload. SSR worker locale Since 0.3.0 the Node SSR worker pins its locale deterministically so , , , and similar APIs produce the same strings on every host. Without this, the worker inherits whatever the process was started with — often on CI, on EU servers, in containers — and any serverrendered formatted date immediately mismatches what the browser produces, tripping React hydration. Default: , . Override both via the environment variable before Pyxle boots: The worker only sets these if the surrounding environment hasn't already — so a systemd unit or container image that pins its own still wins. If a page genuinely needs userlocaleaware formatting, render those pieces inside instead; SSR emits a placeholder and the client fills it in after mount, avoiding the mismatch entirely. Performance tips Use worker pool mode () for production. Start with N = number of CPU cores. Keep loader functions fast. The loader runs on every request cache expensive calls. Avoid heavy imports in the Python section. Imports are loaded every time the module is hotreloaded in dev mode. Minimise HEAD callable complexity. Dynamic HEAD functions run synchronously on the main thread.

searchIndex.description: The Pyxle compiler transforms `.pyxl` files into separate Python and JSX artifacts. This document explains how the compilation process works.
searchIndex.searchText: Compiler Internals The Pyxle compiler transforms files into separate Python and JSX artifacts. This document explains how the compilation process works. Compilation overview When you run or , the compiler: 1. Scans the directory for files 2. Parses each file to separate Python from JSX 3. Writes serverside Python modules to 4. Writes clientside JSX modules to 5. Generates layout composition wrappers in 6. Creates a Vite configuration at The parser The parser () is ASTdriven — there are no fence markers, string directives, or perline keyword heuristics. It finds the Python/JSX boundary by walking the source and, at each position, growing the largest region that parses as valid Python via ; when Python stops parsing, it grows a JSX segment until valid Python resumes. How the split works The boundary is decided by what parses as Python, not by the leading keyword. is not valid Python, so it lands in JSX; is, so it stays in Python. Arbitrary alternation is supported (), including JSXfirst files. Inside a JSX segment the parser tracks JS structural state (string and template literals, block comments, brace/paren/bracket depth), so Pythonlooking text inside a JSX body or template literal is never misclassified. What the parser extracts Artifact Description All Python lines concatenated All JSX lines concatenated function metadata (name, line number, parameters) function metadata (name, line number, parameters) Static variable content Whether is a callable JSX blocks extracted for serverside use component props component props Validation The parser enforces (raising a compiletime error otherwise): At most one loader per file and functions must be / must be defined at module scope (not nested) Their first parameter must be named function names must be unique within the file Code generation Server module () The compiled Python module contains: Client module () The compiled JSX module contains: Composed route module () When layouts exist, the compiler generates a wrapper: Vite configuration The compiler generates that: Configures for JSX transforms and React Refresh Sets the to the build directory Maps import aliases for Injects environment variables via Vite's option Incremental compilation During , the file watcher triggers recompilation only for changed files: 1. The watcher detects a file change in 2. Only the changed file is recompiled 3. The server module is reimported (with module cache invalidation) 4. Vite's HMR picks up the clientside changes automatically Build artifacts After , the output structure is: The maps each route to its clientside assets:

searchIndex.title: Frequently Asked Questions
searchIndex.description: Pyxle is a Python-first full-stack web framework. It combines Python server logic with React components in `.pyxl` files, providing file-based routing, server-side rendering, and a development experie
searchIndex.searchText: Frequently Asked Questions General What is Pyxle? Pyxle is a Pythonfirst fullstack web framework. It combines Python server logic with React components in files, providing filebased routing, serverside rendering, and a development experience inspired by Next.js. How is Pyxle different from Next.js? Next.js uses JavaScript/TypeScript for both server and client code. Pyxle uses Python for server logic (data fetching, mutations, API routes) and React for the UI. If your team prefers Python for backend work but wants React for the frontend, Pyxle bridges the gap. How is Pyxle different from Django or Flask? Django and Flask are backend frameworks that render templates. Pyxle is a fullstack framework that: Uses React for the UI instead of template engines Provides serverside rendering (SSR) with clientside hydration Colocates server logic and UI in the same file Includes a bundler (Vite) for hot reload and optimised builds Offers filebased routing instead of URL configuration How does Pyxle compare to Reflex, NiceGUI, or Streamlit? Those frameworks let you build the UI in pure Python; Pyxle has you write real React/JSX — so the entire npm React ecosystem is available — colocated with your Python. The honest tradeoffs, and when each of them is the better choice, are laid out in the full framework comparison. Is Pyxle productionready? Pyxle is in beta (the series). The core features are implemented and tested (2,500+ tests, with a 95% coverage gate enforced in CI), but the API may change before 1.0. Use it for new projects and experiments, but be prepared for breaking changes. What Python version do I need? Python 3.10 or later. Python 3.12 is recommended. What Node.js version do I need? Node.js 20.19 or later (Vite 7's minimum). Node 18 is endoflife and no longer supported. files Can I use TypeScript in files? The JSX section is plain JSX — TypeScript syntax (type annotations, , generics, casts) isn't supported there, and the compiler flags it with a clear, sourcelocated error if you try. You still get typed editor support and a gate against the generated declarations. See the TypeScript guide for the full picture. Can I import Python code in the JSX section? No. The Python and JSX sections are compiled into separate files. Python code runs on the server; JSX runs on both server (SSR) and client. Data flows from Python to JSX through the loader's return value. Can I use any React library? Yes. Any npm package that works with React 19 and Vite should work. Install it via and import it in your JSX section. Can I have multiple loaders in one file? No. Only one function per file. You can have multiple functions. Routing How do I create a 404 page? Create : How do I create a catchall route? Use : How do I exclude a folder from routing? Wrap it in parentheses: creates the route , not . Data loading My loader is slow. Can I cache results? Yes. Pyxle has a builtin page cache: return a envelope from your loader, and the rendered page (loader result and SSR output) is served from cache until it goes stale, then refreshed in the background — so a slow loader runs at most once per window: Only cache pages that render no peruser data — a cached render is shared with every visitor. Loaderless pages opt in with a modulelevel directive, and prerenders them at build time (SSG/ISR). See the Caching guide. To cache just an expensive call inside a loader, a plain on the helper — or an async cache like — still works: Can I access the database from a loader? Yes. Loaders are standard async Python functions. Use any async database library (asyncpg, databases, SQLAlchemy async, Tortoise ORM, etc.). What happens if my loader throws an unhandled exception? If you raise , the nearest is rendered. Other exceptions render a default error page. In dev mode, the error overlay shows the full stack trace. Styling Do I have to use Tailwind CSS? No. Tailwind is optin — asks whether you want it. Decline it and a fresh project uses plain CSS and CSS Modules out of the box; you can also add Sass or any CSSinJS library. Choose it and you get Tailwind v4 wired into Vite. See Styling. How do I add a global CSS reset? Add it to the config: Or link it in your root layout's : Deployment How do I deploy Pyxle? 1. Run to compile assets 2. Run to start the production server 3. Place behind a reverse proxy (Nginx, Caddy) for TLS See Deployment for Docker examples and detailed instructions. Can I deploy to serverless platforms? Pyxle requires a persistent server for SSR (Node.js workers). It is best suited for containerbased deployments (Docker, Kubernetes) or VPS hosting. Serverless platforms that support longrunning processes may work, but this is not tested. How do I set environment variables in production? Use environment variables or a file: Security Is CSRF protection enabled by default? Yes. The doublesubmit cookie pattern is active for all , , , and requests. The component and hook handle tokens automatically. How do I disable CSRF for webhooks? Exempt specific paths: Are my environment variables safe? Variables without the prefix never appear in clientside code. Only variables are injected into JavaScript bundles. Troubleshooting fails with "npm install required" Run or in your project directory. The page renders without styles Vite compiles every stylesheet you import from a JSX module, so the most common cause is a stylesheet that isn't imported anywhere. Make sure your CSS (e.g. ) is imported from a page or layout. If you enabled Tailwind, confirm that entry contains . See the styling guide. Hot reload is not working 1. Check that Vite is running (look for the Vite URL in the terminal output) 2. Check the browser console for WebSocket connection errors 3. Try restarting SSR fails with "Unable to load page module" The compiled Python module could not be imported. Check: 1. Syntax errors in the Python section of your file 2. Missing Python dependencies (run ) 3. Import errors in your function Actions return 403 Forbidden CSRF token mismatch. If you are making manual fetch calls (not using or ), include the CSRF token header. See Security.

searchIndex.title: Changelog
searchIndex.description: Release notes for Pyxle. While we're in beta (`0.x`), minor versions may include breaking changes — those are called out explicitly here. To upgrade, run `pip install --upgrade pyxle-framework`.
searchIndex.searchText: Changelog Release notes for Pyxle. While we're in beta (), minor versions may include breaking changes — those are called out explicitly here. To upgrade, run . 0.7.3 — 20260712 Fix: no longer hotreloads in an endless loop when a global stylesheet or script lives under . is served live and indexonly — it is deliberately never rebuildwatched, because the standalone compiler rewrites and treating that write as a source edit reopens the selfsustaining reload loop fixed in 0.7.0. But the watcher also rebuildwatches the parent directory of every configured global stylesheet/script, and that guard only skipped the directory when it was exactly — so registering a global stylesheet at (a common setup: inject the compiled Tailwind CSS on every page) put back on the rebuild watch and the loop returned, rebuilding several times a second with no edits. Very visible on Linux, where inotify reports every write; masked on macOS by FSEvents coalescing. The globalstylesheet and globalscript guards now skip any directory at or under (), matching the guard — so a public/ global asset is served live like the rest of and no longer drives a rebuild. Verified with a real inotify watch: one seed edit went from 47 spurious rebuilds in 2.5s to 0. Docs: the clientside shape of an 's result is now spelled out — no more confusion. An action's awaited result is the flat : your returned fields sit directly on it (), and a successful result has no . Because separately exposes a property (the last successful return), it was easy — for a person or an AI coding agent — to write , which is on success and silently noops inside , so a mutation looked like it did nothing until the page reloaded. The scaffolded , the Server Actions guide, and the client API reference now state the contract explicitly and warn against , and the README drops an ambiguous spread. No behavior change — the shipped TypeScript types already modelled this correctly. 0.7.2 — 20260711 Fix: scaffolds an installable again. The template pinned — the exact range 0.7.1's security bump moved off — so a fresh project's was unsatisfiable against the line beside it (which requires ), and failed to resolve. The scaffold now pins , matching the framework. Config: a boolean port is now rejected instead of silently binding port 1. Because is a subclass of in Python, slipped past the port validator and bound port (or ). now rejects booleans with a clear , matching every other integer setting in the config. 0.7.1 — 20260710 Security: require Starlette ≥ 1.3.1. Pyxle was pinned to , holding it on 0.37.2 — which has known advisories including a header URLreconstruction auth bypass (PYSEC2026161), unbounded formparsing DoS (PYSEC2026249, PYSEC20261943), and a Windows UNC SSRF/credential leak (CVE202648818). The dependency is now , which fixes all of them. Pyxle's own API surface is unchanged; upgrade with . (Apps that import Starlette internals directly should review the Starlette 1.0 release notes.) A new CI job guards against dependency regressions going forward. refuses to start in production without . Serving without a signing secret meant CSRF tokens fell back to unsigned doublesubmit and every signed cookie (sessions, unsubscribe links) was forgeable — previously only a single easilymissed startup warning. now fails fast with an actionable message and a oneliner to generate a key. still allows a missing key (it uses a public dev fallback), and the check is skipped if you've deliberately set . See Security and Deployment. / now check your Node.js version up front instead of failing later as an opaque Vite crash. If Node.js is missing or older than the required (the Vite 7 floor), the command stops immediately with a clear, actionable message — — rather than printing "ready" beside a red Vite fatal and crashlooping. warns about a belowfloor toolchain after scaffolding (nonfatal, since scaffolding needs no Node), and warns when serving a prebuilt app on an old Node. The dev server also no longer prints its "ready" banner if Vite exits during startup. Installation gains a Troubleshooting section mapping the two most common toolchain errors — pip's "No matching distribution found" (Python below 3.10) and the Node.js version error — to the fix, and Quick Start restates both version floors up front. A real testing story: helpers and a Testing guide. "How do I test my app?" now has a runnable answer. A file isn't an importable Python module, so the old advice () raised . New compiles the page and returns its loader as a plain function you can call with a fake request; returns the whole module so you can reach any by name. A new Testing guide covers unittesting loaders/actions, the keeplogicinplainmodules pattern, and endtoend tests over HTTP (with the CSRF handshake), and the runtime internals doc no longer shows the broken import. A firstclass inprocess is called out as the next step on the roadmap. New Debugging guide, and a more accurate Deployment guide. The Debugging guide covers both runtimes — reading the error overlay, / in a loader, server logs in the browser console, the SSR (Node) side (, running twice, the Node inspector), and reading the compiled output. Deployment fixes: the sample Dockerfile installs Node.js 22.x (the old can ship a belowfloor 20.16–20.18 that Vite 7 rejects); the metrics endpoint is documented at its real path (optin via ), not ; a new Database migrations section covers 's startup/ flow and backwardcompatible migrations for zerodowntime deploys; and a bluegreen gotcha callout explains that the two instances must share an explicit and the same or midcutover POSTs . Security docs now reflect what Pyxle actually sends. The Security guide previously told you to add , , and at your proxy — but Pyxle already sets all three on every response (and uses , not the the old docs showed). The guide now documents the autoset baseline and covers only the two headers genuinely left to you: (appspecific) and (belongs at your TLS layer). A vulnerabilitydisclosure policy () is now published for every Pyxle repository. A fresh project now ships a human . scaffolded an for AI agents but no README for people; new projects get a README covering prerequisites, the run commands, and the project layout. The comparison guide now names what Pyxle doesn't have yet. A new "What Pyxle doesn't have yet" section honestly lists the current edges — authoring the client in TypeScript (endtoend Python types work today; TSX authoring is the next milestone), a firstclass test client, automatic image byte and fontoptimization, and i18n — with how to bridge each one now, so the gaps read as roadmap rather than surprises. 0.7.0 — 20260709 Fix: renders even when an ancestor layout has a loader. The error boundary is compiled wrapped in its layout chain like any page, but its render received no layout loader data — the layout component crashed on the missing props and the request silently fell back to the builtin error document (in dev and production, with no log). Boundary renders now execute ancestor layout loaders exactly like a normal page; a loader that fails during the boundary render no longer masks the boundary (it renders with erroronly props and the failure is logged), and a boundary that itself fails now logs the fallback instead of swapping silently. Also: honest scope wording for (page JSX isn't checked without — planned, see the typedPyxle roadmap), the pyxleauth doc header says 0.4.0, the seed claim matches real SSR behavior, the field is documented, six missing guides join the docs index, and three dead anchors are fixed. Prerelease verification fixes. now actually reaches the SSR worker (the sanitized spawn environment stripped it, making the documented knob a noop). to — what process managers and IDE stop buttons send — tears down the whole child tree (Vite, esbuild, SSR workers) exactly like CtrlC instead of orphaning Vite on its port. A failed rebuild now broadcasts to the browser overlay (as the architecture docs promise),
